I was like, what was that? He was like, oh, I got what I needed. And I just went to go do it. And so he's obviously become very successful since then. And I just remembered that little moment, which is if you get what you need, I would rather you take action while you're motivated than wait until the motivation disappears. Because we have very large amounts of motivation for very small periods of time. So I'd encourage you to take advantage of it. But I'm going to try and do my very best to solve as many problems for as many of you as I possibly can today. All right. And so here's a couple of preframes that I thought were valuable. So one of the reasons that you're not getting as many leads as you want is fundamentally because not enough people know about your stuff. And it's very easy for me to say this, but it's like you have to really internalize how unbelievably large the market is and how small and an ephemeral are attempts at being known are. And so I'll tell you what happened at the last launch two years ago. I had people still coming up to me on the street and 19 out of 20 who'd come up to me and say, hey, you come to the book launch. And they'd be like, I didn't even know you wrote a book. And I'd already had offers out at that point. And I was, you know, advertising my face off last launch and no one knew. Now, this time I had a slightly higher percentage, probably like one of four had heard that I had a book launch coming in three out of four still didn't. And I say that having spent like four or five million dollars. And these are people who already know who I was still didn't even know that I had a book launch coming. And again, I bring this up because so I so many times I hear the like, I think I've saturated my market. I think I don't think there's any more people who could find out. And it's like, you're just so wrong. And I just I want to give as many of examples to blow that blow that belief out of the water as I possibly can. And so to further reinforce this, you guys may not know this. But a month before the leads launch last year, we actually had a leak. And so what happened was one of the people in my team was kind of getting everything ready on Amazon to launch the book. Obviously, you know, release it. And the way Amazon books works, I won't bore you with it, but you have to basically upload the book ahead of time. And they accidentally set it up as live. And so they didn't realize that they had done that. And so for 24 hours, my leads book, which I had been keeping in secret and I've been, you know, preparing all this like whisper, t-shirt, all this stuff, 72 people bought the book in like 60 minutes. And I was, you know, a month plus out and I was like, the launch is ruined. You know, like, oh, like, like the book's going to be out there. Like the whole thing, right? Here's the funniest part of all of it. Nothing happened. And I'll tell you, I'll tell you what gave me peace at night was I realized that somebody would have to take the book and then they would have to begin promoting it. They would have to start advertising it because I was out here shouting from the rooftops that my book was coming out. And it was this big event, this big thing and still 19 out of 20 people had no idea who followed me that I even had a book. And so this, these 72 random strangers who did actually buy the book, had a type. I saw one guy commented on a LinkedIn post. He was like, how was this somehow already in my Kindle? And I was just like, no one comment on this guy's saying. And but as a result, though, it was this really, it was this very valuable lesson for me of like, no one knows you exist. No one knows your business exists. No one knows what you're up to. Everyone's obsessed with their own lives. It's like, we have to put so much effort into becoming known that we just typically wildly under underestimate the effort. That's all I can say is that like, I knew that it would be a lot of work to build a brand that we have now. And it still pales in comparison to how much work it's actually been. And I've told the Ford story before, but I'll just tell this. Actually, I'll skip that and I'll save it for later. And so when we think about this kind of larger scale, I reverse engineered about how much it costs to become kind of a list for kind of a list famous, right? So I would say like, like, she's forgetting the girl's name from Hunger Games. What's her name? Jennifer Lawrence. There you go. So it's clearly not that famous. So Jennifer Lawrence, right? And Jennifer Lawrence, I'll tell you why I made a case study for myself about Jennifer Lawrence and you're like, how is this going to relate to my business? I promise this will all, this will all hopefully elegantly tie together. Jennifer Lawrence was unique for me in that she wasn't very well known before the Hunger Games. And the Hunger Games was largely about her. And so then I looked into how much it cost to advertise the Hunger Games as a movie, right? It was about 50 million bucks. And I took that as almost a placeholder of she went from almost obscure to famous off of $50 million of advertising budget. And so $50 million of advertising budget is roughly five billion impressions. And so if you're like, well, how much does it really cost to make me or make my business famous? It's about $50 million or five billion impressions. So you can either grind or pay, but you get there either way. But that's the amount of money or effort it takes for that to occur. And so once I realized that I was like, whoa, so if you spend $500,000 a year, it's like, okay, it'll take 100 years for, for me to get this level of acclaim. But the issue there, of course, is that it would be concentrated, let's say it's a local marketplace or it's repeated to the same customers, right? You can still cut that down and narrow it from like, I want to be famous globally. I want to be famous nationally. I just want to be famous within my pond. And you can scale that math down to your market. So I just use that as my hypothetical extreme and then reverse it. And for me, probably the United States is the, I would say the primary to our English speaking world is probably the primary market for me. But that, that was the math that I actually did before I got into this. And so this leads me to a very important point that I mean I touched on last time, but I think is worth reinforcing, which is that unless you have a viral products, you have some sort of zero cost software that, that, you know, has no cost to add another person. All growth is constrained by irrelevance. If every single person in the world knew about your business, your business would be bigger than it is right now. If every single person in the world knew about your business, then you would have the talent that you need to grow that business. And so fundamentally, whether your supply or demand constrained, your growth is being limited by the fact that you are irrelevant within the scheme of the large, like the larger universe, right? And I just try to remember this as like, I can probably solve this by reaching out to more people, by making more stuff for the people that I want to serve, and by advertising more. And so I'll give you a final little frame here that I think will be helpful, which is, I had a gentleman, Brent, who has a roofing company, and I said, you know, he needed to get, I think, another 40 sales reps for his business to hit the goals that he had. And it sounded like it was going to be like a year long project or something like that. And after talking to him more about it, I was like, you have kids, right? And he said, yeah. I said, okay, imagine your kids are going to get abducted the end of this month unless you have 40 sales guys. He was like, yeah. I was like, okay, what would you do? What would it take? Because you can do that. And I told this story because Layla and I have a mutual friend who's, who's a many, I think a many-deck billionaire. And when he started this new venture that he raised $30 billion for, fun fact, to build out the first level of team, he flew every single person into a warehouse where he did 300, he hired 300 people in seven days. And they did like 2,000 interviews with a group of 15 people. And I bring this up because when I heard that story, it was just such violent effort that I just am reminded about how much more I need to do to get to where I want to go. And of course, I'm going to get into the tactics and stuff, but like, I think that these are the more important things that can shift your perspective that, that I can offer. And so, like the ACQ implementation workshop, today's behavior changes. So number one, the goal of today is to ensure getting more leads is the best use of your resources. Because fundamentally, the only point of doing it is, is if it's the best return on your time. If you have other things that you could be doing, go do that. And I really mean it. Like, eventually this might be your problem, but it might not be the problem today. And if it's not, then go do the problem. The second is, if it is the problem, then I'll show you the best ways that I know how to advertise. And then number three, help you pick the right path to execute on this. And you'll see that there's a common theme today that there's many ways up the mountain. And I'll talk about this at length in a minute. But fundamentally, there's a lot of ways to solve a problem. And I'm going to show you many different solutions to the same problem. And so the idea of like, is it right or is it wrong? It should probably get out of your vocabulary and you should be thinking in likelihoods and returns. So it's not like this is the right or the wrong advice. It's more, how much more likely is this to work? And what is my adjusted return on each of these different paths? And I think it's a much stronger frame. And it also allows you to entertain even more wild ideas than a more binary thinking process of like yes or no. So I would just pause at that, put your continuum hat on, your returns hat on, rather than your yes, no hat on. So these are supposed to come out one at a time, but that's apparently my mess up. So we'll just pretend the first line came up and we'll just go down with it. So a couple of principles that I think are worth repeating because I end up repeating these so many times when I have L3 days when I meet with entrepreneurs who have this constraint that I figured I would just say them kind of upfront because these can preload all the actions that are going to come later. So number one is that we need to be reminded more than we need to be taught and we need to repeat more than we need to innovate. And so it's one of these very frustrating things that we think because we're so special and we're so creative. We thought of this great idea. It's why so many people are like, he copied my idea. It's like, yeah, in the world of execution, ideas are not worth very much. Right? And so it's so much more about taking huge and unrelenting amount of action for extended periods of time that you actually can get even a small modicum of people to even know you exist. And I bring up that first point if we need to be reminded more than we need to be taught because there may be some things that you hear today that you have heard before. But it's just that today it's the right time to hear it. And so I would, again, I would throw out the like, this is new. This is not new. And try and replace it with, is this what I need right now? And if it is, then it's the most valuable thing you can get. So the second is, and this is probably a very consistent thing within my content, which is that volume negates luck. But if today the problem that you have is that you simply just don't know how to get more early to get them cheaper, my hope is that we solve that plentifully by the end of the day, okay? Another little quote that I like is that, if necessity is the mother of invention, repetition is the father of skill. So like sometimes you need to do more not just because not nearly enough people know about it, but you need to do more because you've got to get better. And the only way to get better is to do more. And so more is almost always the answer to your problems. And even when you don't think it is, it's still probably more. So that's just kind of a quantity versus quality perspective. The next is that fundamentally when I'm looking at someone's advertising or their outreach or their content, you need to make sure that you make it about them and it's not about you. And you have to use their words, not your own. And so one of the most valuable things you can do now with like AI, you can go and research the forums, you can go and research the reviews of competitors' products, of your own products, and then pull out the sentiment, right? And I'll say some project that we just actually did internally, we just reviewed all of our sales calls with AI and were able to identify what actions had the highest likelihood of resulting the sale and what were the actual most common pain points, not the ones we thought, but the ones that actually proceeded purchasing. And they were actually different than I expected. And so we're now going to launch a new campaign with each of the pain angles that is most commonly being brought up in people who buy. Brilliant, right? But like, it sounds obvious, right? And so like a lot of this marketing who do, I think gets, it distracts people from what matters, which is like, you just need to be very specific and say, you said you want this thing, I can help you with this thing by buying my thing. That's it. And everything else is just kind of like distracts from the core message, this core dollars, you know, eyeballs in, dollars out. And so I like this little pithy aphorism, but like, assume they have no idea who you are, what you do, how it works, they're in a rush, and they have a third grade education, right? We need to keep it specific over general. And we need to think about the benefit of the benefit. So what do I mean by that? So these are very large kind of marketing concepts. But I bring these up because when I look at most people's marketing, these are the things that are missing. I'll give you all these tactics. I'll give you all these things. But like, if these are wrong, those won't matter. Like if you aren't doing enough or you can't stick with a marketing channel long enough, or you think that you're doing 10 outreaches a week is sufficient, or you're making, you know, five pieces of content a week is somehow going to like grow the business. It's like you just have a wildly mismatched expectation against reality. And then there's a whole another group of people who you have to be specific. You have to be clear. And a lot of times this happens in a little bit more marketing-centric entrepreneurs. They think there's so much magic in it. And I feel like some of you might have seen this before, but one of my favorite memes of all time is something called the mid-wit meme, which looks like this. And you've kind of got like, you know, like an idiot over here. And then you've got like a, you know, smart person here. Maybe I'll put some glasses on him, right? And then you've got like the mid-wit. And so what happens is you just want to make sure that you're not this guy, because usually this guy and this guy agree, and then this is the one that gets everyone confused. It's like, how many seconds should my ad be? And at what point should I drop this color? I think it's because the buttons changed and vibrated. And this guy's like, I think you just got to tell people what you have and make sure they understand that it's for them. And this guy's like, just tell them what you have and make sure that they understand it's for them. Right? So you end up going through this whole cycle just to end up with like, just be clear. Get to the point. And so many times I've seen this happen, but deletion will often beat explanation. Do we really need to tell them about it or can we just remove it? I would say nine times out of ten when we look at a portfolio of salescript, if it's underperforming, we remove stuff. We cut stuff. We just make it shorter. And believe it or not, when you make it shorter, you can be very clear about what point we're trying to actually make. And if you don't know what the point of the salescript is, that's the problem. And if you don't know the point of the ad, that's also the problem. And so a lot of the last piece of this, if I were to break this into three chunks, first is that you need a shitload of volume. The second is you need to be clear. You need to be specific and not fancy. And you speak in their language. And the third kind of theme here is around proof. Proof is still, if I had overrated and underrated, proof, despite all of the praise it gets, is still underrated. You need to show more. You need to document more. You need to show more than you currently are. I'm going to give you a bunch of tactics on how to weave this into your business. But you want to say what only you can say and you want to show what only you can show. The reason that the marketing that I have, I believe, is so effective is because you've probably noticed this. I want to demonstrate always. How can I show first? Because if I could just stand next to my 20,000 five-star reviews, I don't need to say much. I can be like, the book's good. Look. And if you have a history of seeing stuff that has 20,000 five-star reviews being good, then you'll probably just believe me. Because fundamentally, the biggest issue in marketing is that no one believes. And so everything we do is to accomplish that one objective. No one believes you. If you're like, I can help people decrease their page load times. Everybody would want faster page load times. So why don't people buy from you? They don't believe you. That's it. They don't believe you. And so just putting that as the top of the pyramid before you get into everything else, before you get into all the fancy copy, before you get into all the selling, before all the tactics, before all the money models, everything, it's like if you don't have enough leads, it's because no one believes you. And they don't believe you because you haven't shown them or given them a reason to. And so the number one rule of marketing that we have at Acquisition.com, and every company I have, it's almost like this, you know, bold, I would have like it would be above if we had just one office, we had more than enough people for that. But if we had just one door that marketing was under, it would be state the facts and tell the truth. Kind of like Dante's Inferno, it says, abandon all hope ye who enter here, right? So state the facts and tell the truth and be the thing over the door. And the magic of that statement is that if the facts and the truth are not compelling, you don't warp them, you warp reality, you change your environment, you change what you want to show. And basically that means maybe you need to do more, maybe you need to get those 20,000 five-star reviews. Because that's going to be the thing that when you just say, hey, I have 20,000 five-star reviews, you will get everything else you need. The reason I didn't make a ton of general business content until I had a, you know, $46 million exit was because I was like, who would listen to me? Even though I had Jim Wanch and I at Prestige Labs, I still didn't think that anyone would listen to me until I had some sort of tangible proof that I was legit, right? And so many of you are just missing that proof. And the thing is, is that proof doesn't even take that long to get, you're just not getting it. So go get it. And so that leads into the final point about brand, which I think, I think I'm going to save this to the end and we'll get into some tactics now. But if you guys want, you're the end, if you guys still have brain juice, I'll talk about brand stuff because at the end of the day, brand is going to be the thing that's going to help you continually compound your lead flow, continue to get more customers who want to spend more money with you, who do more referrals, they have more repeat purchases. All of this is the result of brand. It just takes a very long time to build. And so I want to get you guys leads today. But the long, the kind of thing of like, all the things I'm going to tell you today are things that are kind of like a paycheck. You do the work and you get paid. Brand is kind of like a 401k or an investment where you have to put money into it and you don't really see anything for a while until eventually the passive income from your brand is greater than the income you get from your regular work. And at that point, the brand becomes bigger than what you can do any day. But it takes time, just like an investment. And most people don't save and most people don't invest and that's because no one can delay gratification. But if you can, and the thing is that a brand takes significantly less time to invest in than trying to build up 40 years of savings. It takes way less time. You can build a really good brand in a couple of years. But most people can't think past this week or this month. So I would encourage you to think a little bit longer and I'll have more taxes on that later. But I just wanted to put these kind of as strategic principles before we dive into the tactics because if you don't do enough, none of this will matter. If you overcomplete the hell of it and you make it about you and instead of making it about them using their words, being specific to their needs, being clear over clever, getting to the point, none of this stuff will matter. And if you don't have enough proof, before you do anything, go get more proof. Like every business I've ever started, I start with proof before I do anything. Because why bother? One, I want to be more convicted to know that I'm really good at this thing. And number two, I want everyone else to be convicted because it's like, why would I waste months trying to sell people when I haven't even proven that I can do it? So go prove you can do it, then the sales will get way easier. And you'll end up one year later being way further ahead of the guy who just tries to sell everyone who comes in with no practice. It's like I can get so much more practice by just doing more reps and documenting them than anything else. Okay, so a few ground rules for today. Some of these things that you have heard, which is a good thing, they may not have been what you needed to hear at that time, but they may be right now. So approach these things with fresh eyes. Beyond that, the clouds versus the dirt dichotomy. So one of the big issues I have is I can go pretty much as deep as you want because I've done all these things. But at the same time, there's many different businesses and industries that are represented here. And so I'm going to go kind of like two layers down and the like click here and do this button stuff. I'm going to assume that you have a certain level of figure it out. Do you even be able to be on this call that you can handle? And if you can't figure enough stuff of like click here, click there type stuff, nothing's going to work anyways and you should try and figure that out. And I really mean that, especially marketing nowadays is almost like a half tech role. I would encourage you to break any beliefs you have around like I'm not a tech person. Like I would stop speaking that over yourself in general because like this is the world we live in and it's only going to get more technical, not less. And so you want to use these tools. And you might be like, Hey, I'm an old school guy. Like that's amazing. I'm glad that you're an old school person. But when you're in the war of attention, the person who uses no tools versus the person one who does, the guy with the tools is going to win. And usually it's not even close. So like if you try to dig a hole with your bare hands, you're not going to dig. Like you could dig a hole. It's not going to be very good. Now consider that compare that guy to a guy who just has a shovel and he can start digging all ways. Going to dig the hole way faster than the guy with his hands and his hands are going to be beat up or nearly as beat up by the end of the day. Now imagine that guy next to a guy with a bulldozer. He's going to dig a way bigger hole in way less time. And the thing is it's the same human. It's just human plus tools and an outcome. And so I would not take any kind of human pride in being old school about this because at the end of the day you just need to win. So here are my two truths for the day. You aren't getting as many leads as you want because number one, people don't know you exist. And number two, the very, very few people who do, you haven't given a good enough reason to engage. That's it. That's fundamentally it. You've got attention. You haven't gotten in front of people. And if you have, you didn't give them enough reason. That's it. And so that's what we're going to fix today. Sorry, I'll tickle. But before we do, I want to make sure that we're solving the correct problem. Nice, cold. Nice. So part one, confirming your mountaintop. So how do you know if leads are truly your constraint? Right? So question number one, can you handle more customers? Yes or no? So in the chat, let me see it. Yes or no, can you handle more customers? Why are, okay. So for everybody who's answering yes, good, because otherwise it would be a short day. So that's question one. So question two is if yes, can you afford to get more? As in like, could you just spend more? And if you spent more, you would get more. All right. So here's what's nasty about what I'm about to share with you. If you answered yes to the first thing and yes to the second, you should just do that. Just go spend more. Just go do more outreach. Just make more content. And you can forget about everything I'm about to talk about. For real. This is like, this is the ultimate like, I don't need you to need me. I want you to win. Now, if you said you can handle more customers and you can't afford to get more, then is it because leads cost too much? And the way that you know that is based on industry averages. Now, if you're not familiar with that, maybe I'll do some, I'll do some calls to the very end of the day. But like, if you're acquiring local businesses, your lead, you know, your cost of acquisition should probably be between $1,500 and $3,500 ish. If you're just looking for normal brick and mortar businesses, home services, things like that, if you sell to that customer, if you're looking at consumers, that's going to vary widely. And it's going to depend on the volume of spend, but it can be anywhere from, and again, it's going to depend on the price point that you're selling at. But from, you know, in the tens of dollars all the way to about a thousand, depending on what it is that you're selling. I'm speaking in broad generalities. And if you don't know how to do this, just Google industry average cost of acquisition. You can also, you know, AI it to figure this out. And the reason this is important, I'll tell you the story, but like Alan, the software company we had, I could see everybody's cost of acquisition. And what was fascinating was that the cost of acquisition between people in different industries was awesome, very similar. But the size of the businesses wasn't. And so the goal of today is really only to fix your business if your cost of acquisition is way too high. Otherwise, you have other problems in the business for real, even though you probably love marketing and maybe you like some of my stuff around marketing. It still might not be the issue. So the next issue is like, okay, maybe you can't afford them because you confer too few. Well, if that's your issue, today I'm not going to solve that. I'm just going to show you how to get more leads for less. That's all I'm going to show you how to do today. The next workshop where I talk about sales conversion, we'll talk about that stuff. But today is just going to be on how to get leads to cost less. And again, how would you know about this? It's going to be benchmarks by conversion mechanism. So you're either going to sell in person with a salesperson, on the phone with a salesperson, via checkout page, or via some sort of big presentation. But there's not that many conversion mechanisms. And so we just need to know what your conversion is compared to benchmarks. And if you're at conversion, then your leads are costing too much. If both of those are right, then we could run the other issues. So here we go. Now, the third issue would be that you don't make enough from them. This is the lifetime gross profit problem. Now, if this is looking familiar to the Mosey 6 or the 6Ms, that's because that's exactly what this is. So if you don't make enough from them, then again, if you have your industry, you have the lead costs, but you're still making money, that's a pretty good sign. And then finally, if you don't have the cash to cover it, then that would be the next issue, which is leads are costing the right amount, we convert the right amount, we still make profit, we're not getting paid fast enough. So today is only going to cover this one. So it's funny because the Mosey 6 is just like these six bullets, technically nine, I think, if you think about all the sub-questions. But this entire presentation for today is literally just around one of those bullets, because there's a lot there and there's a lot of different ways to solve it. So quick side quest on this. If you are not supply constrained, which many of you are not, an increase in any part of the funnel will increase throughput. So I'll give you an example here. Let's say you have $1 CPCs, so cost per clicks, whatever, they go into here and then you get 10% to opt in and you get 10% to schedule. I'm just making these numbers up, these are not good numbers. All right, then you get 10% of those people to buy. All right, just very simple math here. So if this is the case, we would just know, if I can move this to 15%, I would make more money. If I move this to 15% and everything else to save the same, I would make more money. If I move this to 15%, I would make more money. All of these would make more money. Also, by the way, how much more would you make? 50% more, just by changing one of these things, like wild, which is why actually having metrics on each of these things, when you look at them, you're like, I'll bet you I could get this from 10 to 20. Well, there's a double. My scale is right, I could probably get this from 10 to 50. Well, there's a 5x. Well, if we just did those two things, you have a 10x in your business, right? Now, you might think, okay, and the reason I'm bringing this up is, well, if I need to get cheaper leads, wouldn't either of those things result in that? And the answer is yes. The reason this is important to bring up is that there are multiple ways of the mountain. There are multiple ways to solve your problems. And I think there's a huge fallacy among entrepreneurs, or just people in general, to think that there's only one solution. And I think part of that is because the formal education system teaches us that there's only one right answer. But we have to think with a divergent thinking process rather than a convergent thinking process. There's many ways to solve it, and they can also all be right. And the more ways you can solve it rightly, the more right your answer will be in terms of how much you can make from it. Okay, so with that being said, part two, many ways up the mountain. So there are many equally effective ways to decrease lead costs and increase lead flow. All right, so if you imagine each of these kind of colored lines on this mountain here, each of those is another way that could get to the top. All of them start in different reduction, you'd have different scenery, different views. But fundamentally, all of them get to the top. And so you can imagine one person yelling at another person saying, my way of getting the top is better. Now, if we looked at this assuming this is two-dimensional, there are some that will be shorter paths than others. And so there are some paths that may be better for you. And so the path that you would select, or the path that I would select for you, because we're going to go through a lot of different paths, should be the highest risk-adjusted return path based on your existing skills and resources. So that means that two people can look at the mountain, and one person's really good at jogging, and one person's really good at climbing, you know, rock climbing. Both of them might get to the top, and both of them might take the same amount of time, even though both might cover a very different amount of distances. And one might be riskier than the other. But it's all based on your existing skills and resources. So with this being said, and this is the value of like, okay, how can Alex specifically help me? If you were here in person, right, I would look at your background and your skillset and the cash you got on hand, and I'd be like, okay, well, of these three different ways, which one do I think is the highest likelihood of them working? So if you've done ads before, I'd probably recommend if you need to get more leads that you do more better ads. And I'll talk about that in a second. If you've made content before, my likely first suggestion would be to make more better content. If you've done outreach before, then the recommendation might be do more better outreach. Now, of course, there's a time when you start other channels and things like that, but by and large, more is always the risk-adjusted best bet. And so this is kind of how we're going to dive into this and how I've split this up today, because many of you guys acquire customers in different ways, right? But fundamentally, almost everyone here requires customers through ads, outreach, or content, or a combination of them, all right? Once we know that, we'll then need to pick the right method, right, which is what we just covered. And once you pick the right method, next we'll have to pick the right strategy, more better new. Ta-da. 99% of the time, it's going to be more better and not new, just as a side note. And if you think, by the way, about how this nets out to volume times leverage equals output, which is the ultimate work equation for what you want, more is volume, leverage is getting more out of the volume, which is better, and then that gets you what you want, which is output. So if you want to increase output, you either got to do more or do it better. Or my personal favorite, do both. Now, what I have noticed over my career, because I'm somebody who will take a huge amount of action for extended periods of time, is that there tends to be this kind of accordion, this kind of accordion back and forth that has to be more and better. So I'll give you an example. So let's say that you are making, you know, one YouTube video a week, and it's generating you X amount of business or growth. Okay. If you make more YouTube videos, and you go to five YouTube videos a week, you'll likely get more views than you did with one. But then when you look at your five YouTube videos, you'll probably then notice that one of the five or one of your 10 will dramatically outperform the other nine. And then you'll think, man, if I just had more time, I'll bet you I could make more of those other nine like that one. And so then you say, well, what if we went from 10 videos down, you know, every two weeks to three? And then you make three videos. And then those videos are on average significantly better, and then you end up getting more views overall. But then once you get really good at making those three, you're like, you know what, let's say if we can do 10 like that every two weeks, and then you go back out. And then all of a sudden one of those 10 is going to be way better than the other nine. Right. So this is an accordion process that is normal and to be expected. And I think that it just depends on where you're at kind of in the pendulum swing. But this is just something that I have noticed is that we do more, we do better, and we do better, and we do more, and we go back and forth with this. But you still have to be making sure that you're doing one or the other. And if you're not sure, start with more. Because more will always make you better. And so there's also like a math reason behind this, which is for most people risk is, sorry, for most people more is the risk adjusted best move. Like what you're doing is already working to a degree. It's a simply just adding significantly more volume into that, into our little process here that has money spit out the other side. There's so little risk of doing more, like that risk doesn't work. It's almost always the one worth exhausting first. And it's just that people have these very odd limiting beliefs around like, well, I can't do more than 100 outreaches a day. It's like there are companies that do 100,000 outreaches a day, right? But you may be like, well, I don't know how to get the leads for that. Great. At least you can identify what your problem is. Now you can go solve it. So I'll give you a broad sweeping generalization here that's almost always right. So if you are less than a million dollars a year, the answer is almost always more. And the reason that you have volatility right now in your business, like sales go up and down, right, is that it's a product of insufficient volume. You're small because you do too little and no one knows you exist. And so I'll give you the typical example here. Let's say you do one deal a month, all right? And next month you'll do one deal. You don't know a day of the week, but usually do about one deal a month. And the next week, sorry, the next month you'll do another deal. And you don't know where it's going to come from, but you'll do one deal, right? There is a math way of expressing this, which is that every day you do one 30th of your daily advertising activity. And so what appears to be volatility is just insufficient volume. You're just not doing enough. And so you have to raise your minimum standard of how much that is, right? So if you wanted to get to a deal a day, and there are probably people in your industry who can do a deal a day, whatever industry you're in, said differently, there are people in your industry, almost every one of you here, that there's somebody doing 30 times more sales than you. That's probably true. And if that is true, I will also bet you they're doing 30 times or more the advertising activity. And that sounds crazy to say because you're looking at it from your own perspective. But it's true. Now, I want to put again some perspective on this. So this is from the recent book launch. People saw the $100 million number and were like, wow, that was a big number, right? We also had more than 20,000 affiliates. So think about 20,000 different businesses that were promoting this launch on our behalf. If I just said that, people would be like, whoa, that would be a really big launch. Now, if I also said that we made over 3,000 ads that we ran in the six-week window, that would also be something that people would be like, whoa, if we only ran 3,000 ads, that would be a crazy amount of advertising that we'd be doing that period of time. Now, if I also said we advertised those 3,000 ads across, and this is not counting doubles, this is just counting creative, but we took those 3,000 and then multiplied that again across X, YouTube, Instagram, Facebook, TikTok, Podcasts, Email, billboards, direct mail. A lot, a lot. And if I just said that we were running campaign across all of those advertising mediums, you'd probably be like, whoa, that's a ton. But then on top of that, we made 35,000 pieces of content. And so I'm demonstrating this so violently because I think that if I can break this belief, if I just break this belief, then I'll have like, if I can just change your behavior in this way, that you just dramatically increase how much your standard is for how much you need to do, then like mission accomplished. But you probably just aren't doing enough. And so you have to start counting in thousands, not in ones and tens. And I use the rule of 100 in the Leeds book as just like a primer for somebody who's getting started. But like, if you're trying to win, like, the nice thing is that no one takes any action, and so like it's very easy to beat people, but you just need to make sure that you're not one of them, which is most people. And so I'll tell you something that kind of shifted my perspective in terms of, you're increasing a minimum standard. So in the financial world, some of you guys may know this, if you're in the high finance world where they say finance. All right, if you know what I'm saying, it's a very small, it's inside you. Anyways, but if you're at the Goldman Sachs's of the world, if you're in the private equity world, right, one of the sayings that they'll have is like, here's a stick or I made two sticks, right. Now what is a stick? A stick is slang for a million dollars. And because those guys make so much money that they change the increment back to one, one stick, two sticks, four sticks, right, that's how much money they made. And I remember when I was broker that I used to measure the amount of money I had in Chipotle burritos, because I just knew that that's how many meals I could have from that. As I made a little bit more money, it was months of rent, right, and you probably have some standard that will continually increase. And so this is where like you can tell typically how wealthy someone is by the increments they count in in terms of time for how long they think out and money in terms of how they think about their bets. And so right now, like if I were to start a new business, and this is again minimum standard thing, if I was going to get into a new industry, right, I'm immediately going to be thinking like how do we build a 50 to 100 person sales team? How do we get our, how can we scale our spend up to a few hundred thousand a day? And I'm basically just going to be relentlessly going in that direction until we've at least hit there, because that's where I'm very, very comfortable. Now, the thing is that there's guys who are bigger than me that are like, if we're not spending a billion month in advertising, like what are we doing? Right? Or we're not spending, you know, we're not spending X, Y, Z, like there's levels to this game. And so the goal is like, how can you pull forward your standards of future you to today so you can solve for it and do so much more volume? Now, this is all about more, you're like, but when do you do better? And so the nice thing is that there is a math answer for this too, which is that when the incremental return on better is greater than more. So fundamentally, what is strategy again? Strategy is prioritization of resources, right? And good strategy is getting the highest return on the limited resources you have. That's good strategy, right? And so I'll give you a very simple example of A versus B. So right now, if you're, you know, sub a million, you probably have like one salesperson. And so the effort it takes to hire another salesperson is large, right? But if you have one salesperson, you almost always just need to add more salespeople, right? And so the result of going from one proficient salespeople to two is that you would double sales. Now, let's compare that. That one salesperson, let's assume that they're proficient, right? They're doing okay. Maybe they're not the best in the world, but they're okay, right? You do everything under the sun to that one salesperson. And you might take your current salesman from 25% close rates to 40, which would be a 60% improvement. And that'd be a monster improvement, right? That'd be a great improvement for one guy. But in that instance, it would still probably make sense for you to just get more salespeople. Because if you just doubled them and didn't go from 25% to 40%, you would still make more money. And you'd also decrease the risk of the business because you wouldn't be, you know, limited by one guy. Now, when does it make sense for you to do better? So let's say you have a 20-person sales team and getting them from 25% to 30% as a team might end up being less work than onboarding and training another four salespeople, right? And so if I go from 25% to 30%, that's a 20% improvement. And if I go from 20 salespeople to 24 salespeople, that's also a 20% improvement. And so both of those net you a 20% improvement. The question is which of them is easier? Which of them requires fewer resources? And so that, like that fundamental decision, so if you're asking yourself, like, well, I want to make more money, well, both of those would make you more money. So which one do you do? That is the essence of good strategy. Both would work. The key is that we move faster by getting more for what we put in. And that is the foundation of leverage, which is making the decision or the right decision that will yield you the most for what you put in. So we covered more. We covered better. When do you do new? At least as it relates to getting more leads. I have so dramatically constrained the new bucket. Like, you have to have a very good reason to do new when you could just do more or better. So number one is that if you've actually maximized the market, this is far more common local than it is national, and if you've actually maximized a marketplace, you have a huge percentage of market share, or your spend covers that entire marketplace many times over in terms of frequency, yeah, you can look somewhere else. You can fish in another pond. The next is if the platform itself is shrinking. So let's say, you know, you use newspaper ads as your way of getting customers, but newspapers are going down 25% a month or a year. Well, you know, you can do math that like 25% compounding in the wrong direction is not a good thing. And so getting a head start on getting to another platform, doing something new, probably a good idea. The third situation is where, and this is the one for all of them, is that the incremental returns on new are greater than the returns on more or better, which is super rare, because we have to look at it as risk adjusted. So I know that if I just double my ad spend, like, I've already covered almost all of this risk. So if I double my ad spend, it's likely that I'll have a double and throughput, or at least a significant increase in throughput, right? If I take that same money and put it into something new, the likelihood that that thing does better than me just putting more into my main machine, low. But then you probably ask yourself, well, once I do more than what do I do? It's like, well, then you ask the question of like, can I do more again? And you'd be surprised at how often the answer is yes. And so then the problems that you have to solve are, well, how do I do even more? How do I do even, even more? And these are the hard but solvable problems rather than the exciting problems that lead you to new and shinier objects. So once we've picked the right strategy, then we pick the right tactics. So we figured out our method. We're running ads. We're doing app and we're doing content. We talked about that. Then we figured out we're doing more, better or new. Got that. Then we do tactics, which then goes to part three, which is picking the right path up the mountain. All right. So first we had to confirm, is this even a problem for you? If it's not a problem for you, you should not do this right now. Like this is not the constraint of your business. So let's assume that you said, you know what? It is the constraint of my business. I need cheaper leads specifically. Everything else is fine. I just need cheaper leads. And if I did that, then I would make more. Cool. Under that assumption, then there's many ways that you can go up the mountain. And then you have to understand that concept because any of them could work, but we want to pick the one that has the highest risk adjust return for you based on your existing skills and resources and the path that you choose. Now, we get to picking which path you want to take, which is part three. Okay. So for ads, these are the kind of big six. These are the six bigger buckets of things that can improve your ads. Like I want you to get off on the exit that is where you're going. Like you don't need to just stay on the highway the whole time. Like I want to get you to where you're trying to go. So if you're like, oh, clearly this is my problem, then don't overwhelm yourself with other stuff. Ice cold. So nice. Okay. Let's start with the first one. But despite writing the book about it, many people still have terrible offers and or terrible lead magnets. I forgot to put the little things so that it comes one at a time. So I apologize. So these are all popping up at once. Okay. So let's imagine that and this is actually probably the most common thing. Like this is especially true with home services. But if you were in an industry where it's very difficult for other people to compare your offer, very easy for people to compare your offer to some analysis, you need to win on one of these three vectors. You need to be faster than somebody else. You need to be less risky than somebody else or you need to be easier or multiple. But you have to, from a strategy perspective, you want to focus on at least one of those three things. And so whether you're doing kitchen remodeling, you're doing roofing, you're doing concrete, it doesn't matter. There's always a way to be faster. There's always a reason to be less risky. There's always a way to make it easier for the customer. I promise you there's always a way. Now then you might think, oh, but I can't afford to do that. No shit. That's why you raise your prices to reflect this premium because you're no longer a commoditized product. And guess what? People will pay for it. So what I would encourage you to think about your offer is, what would it take for me to get this outcome for a customer in one third of the time? Like actually answer that question. This is why it's good to do this with your teams. Like what would it really take? So rather than saying we can't do it or we can't afford to do it, answer the damn question. What would it take? Now if it would take paying contractors 50% more to get them to do same day, next day responses for you, fine. Then the next question is, can we sell that to customers? Now if the answer is yes, then ding, ding, ding, we have a winner. Now if the answer is no, then we just go up market to rich people who absolutely value speed, risk and ease. So if you need to approve the offer, one of the first places I look is speed, right? What would it take for us to get it all done in one third of the time? So I know we're getting X rated. Did I talk about this? This is going to be my launch of only Alex. I didn't tell you guys that. The next one is risk, right? So can we ensure the biggest fear or can we guarantee against it? So you can think about this from a warranties perspective. You can think about it from a guarantees perspective or one of my personal favorites, because this is something that I've implemented with plenty of businesses, is you actually just get an insurance policy on whatever their biggest fear is and you say, don't worry, we cover damages up to X because we already have insurance for it. And you include that into your cost, which typically is not nearly as expensive as you think it would be. So that's the second vector that you can consider from an offer's angle. And yes, I'll be here all night and I've got even less underneath. Calm down. So the third is ease. And so the way to think about ease is how much can we kind of prepare the way for the customer ahead of time? Like how can we prepare for them? Is there anything we can do in the intake? Is there anything we can do with the onboarding? Can we do any kind of this prep ahead of time so we can do pre-work for them so we can make their experience that way? Can we make their experience that much more seamless? And what's crazy is that so few businesses think this way or do it, that customers will almost always appreciate and recognize it when you do it in your business. Right? Like this is a soft toss, but for those of you who have been out to L1 or L3 or L2 for in-person, many of you can probably sell like, we take a huge amount of care in making this an exceptional experience for everyone and try to bring in as many of these tiny little details as we possibly can. So like when people know your name when you walk in, they know about your business. Like all that stuff takes work on our side so we can make it a delightful experience for you. And so when we're thinking about the offer, like I think about these value vectors because these are the things that are really going to drive the business. And right now if you're like, I'm suffering because I'm a commodity, this is how you solve it. And so if I have higher row as here and I can reach more customers, it's by far better front end. Which is why I still, it's so funny because I hear people be like lead magnets don't work. It's just like it's small businesses who say this. Right. And small as relative, maybe, maybe the person you listen to is making $5 million a year, whatever. But like everything's relative here. And so it's not right or wrong. If you only are trying to make a smaller amount of money, you for sure will get higher rise if you just say, hey, go buy my stuff. Like there's no question about that. But if you want to get into huge higher realms, you have to soften the ask and make it more attractive for people to take action and then create the conversion process around that. Describe it in their own language of what they want. Right. 11 frameworks we use to build wildly profitable gyms. Helpful. Now there's three types of lead magnets. You can reveal a problem. You can do a trial of the solution or you can do a one step of multi step. All right. This is a splinter offer. So reveal a problem. Seven mistakes homeowners make when fixing their HVACs.
