And so their, their whole lead magnet is we'll just get you one, two or three for free. And then we'll call you in seven days and ask if you still want it. It works great. They're crushing it. All right. Like if you can give a trial of the solution, one of those vibe boys to do it. Super easy. You will get a ton of leads for it. Now the third is the one step of multi step. This is a splinter offer. All right. So I had a cash guys episode where I had a lady named Ashley who did fashion consulting. And so she had a request a quote type thing. Like just book a call with me for an assessment. And it's okay. It's just like, I'd rather have some sort of incentive to do it. Right. And so we came up with, she's going to figure out my, my color palette. Right. Like what are the, what are the colors I should be wearing with my skin tone with my personality with my whatever with my zodiac sign. Who cares? All right. But the point is I'm going to get some, some sort of value from this exchange. So then I asked, okay, how quickly can you automate it? And she's like, yeah, I can auto be pretty quickly. Great. So that's what we can do. And when you do a one step of multi step, everyone like my pushback that I almost always get is everything I do comes together. No, it doesn't. There are always pieces that you do in a process. Well, I'm not going to fix someone's door. It's like, well, you can fix part of it. You can put a coat of paint on, you can put a ceiling on before you, for, for, for, there's always, you can just measure it. You get, there's always some piece that you can pull out and say, I'll do this for free or for a significant discount. And so let's think about it like this. And now, and now that money models is actually out, kind of exciting for me. It's been years. I can talk about this stuff. So let's say that you have an offer. All right. Let's see if my drawing is going to be up to up to stuff here. That's the one up. All right. There we go. Oh, I almost had it. I just messed it up. Okay. So we've got this, this, this offer that has a B C D you got four components to our offer. Right. And let's say you currently sell this for $2,000. It's often more powerful for you to have just take this guy. Move it over here and have. Be over here. That's gone. And then give this way for 90% off. Or free. And then once someone has this, they then want to buy a CD. That's how that works. And so there's almost always a version of this that can get someone in the door. And this is also how you can make what I prefer to have, which is insane discounts. Not like I hate the idea of a 10 or 20% discount. There's no point. Right. I want to have crazy discounts because I want, I want a discount to be significant enough that it changes someone's behavior. And I want to have a discount. I want to have a discount. I want to have a discount. I want to have a discount. I want to have a discount. I want to have a discount. I want to have a discount. And this is why I want to have a discount. Well, that is what I started off with and that's kind of what it costs, perspective. When I think of selling cheapohmino, I got it in my head. Then, when I was selling cheapohmino products, I said, cooker burnedton veer takes, ever business or market you're in. You don't promise anything around speed, you don't promise anything around ease, you don't do anything to reverse risk, and you don't have to do all three to be clear. Just focus on one hammer it and do it really well. And proceeding your offer, you just don't have anything, and you don't give people a good reason to engage in your ads and actually give you their cocktail information. So make something that's really compelling. My personal favorite way of giving lead magnets is to give something that other people charge for. The more real the lead magnet is, the more that it actually has true cost, the more likely it is that it'll work. So that was the offers component for ads. Now I'm not gonna repeat that one for the other two because it's the same thing, but right now I'm bringing this up because so many of you guys, that is your problem. Your offer sucks, your lead magnet sucks. So let's fix that. Now let's think about making more ads, which of course make better ads. So I'm so disappointed that I did my little check marks wrong. So annoyed. Anyways, the benchmark here, and I wanna put this right out the bat, is that right now I don't believe in like, how much, how many ads should I make? It's one of the top questions I get for people who run ads. How many ads should I make for X dollars a month and spent, right? Very common. You should make more ads than you currently are. I'll just leave that there. You should make more ads than you currently are. But if you insist on having some benchmarks you can at least measure yourself, I would say one to five ads per thousand dollars in monthly spend, roughly. All right, and that's if you're national. If you're local, you're gonna do like five to 10 ads per thousand dollars in monthly spend, roughly. Okay, so if you're spending $10,000 a month in your local, you're probably gonna want some of the neighborhood of like 50 to 100 ads a month that you're gonna be making. That's 25-ish ads a week. If you're national, you can typically get away with a little bit less because you don't fatigue your markets as quickly. Believe it or not, local can be tougher because you actually fatigue through the markets faster. Now, I wanna exclude keywords for this if you're a Google PPC type person. Once you find your keywords, you more or less don't change it. Now, those are benchmarks that I'll begin with. The following stuff is the different types of ads. Now, this is directly from GoToDads, so I will bring these up because some of you haven't read it, even though you have it sitting on your desk, please read it. But I'm gonna walk through these quickly so that you can get ideas for how you can make better ads. So, number one is Lifecycle Ads. So, when I was trying to sell Jim Launch, I had to think of other ways that were not with my face to make ads. So I don't include direct-to-camera in this because that's obvious. Let's think about all the other ways you can make ads. All right, so Lifecycle Ad is, you have multiple touch points with a customer, right? So let's say you have a Zoom call. Oh, God, what is it? Is that what a Zoom call looks like? All right, I'm just gonna visit here. These are all Zoom calls. Okay. All right, so you have these Zoom calls. So let's say you have a sales call. You know, you've got sales one, you've got sales two, you've got your onboarding call. Let's say you've got a check-in, hold on, check-in, you know, one through three, all right? So guess what you already have? And most of these are probably recorded already. So this is what you do. You wait until somebody gives you a W and says, I'm awesome, I won. And then you pull up their client profile with all the recordings, and then you find each of their calls, and then you clip together a montage of them going through the sales process to getting onboarded, to getting the result with different outfit, and you put a little timestamp on it. Murders, murders. And the cool thing is you already do this stuff in your business today. You already do this stuff. Now, there's no extra work. You already create this content. You're not using it to advertise. The next is in-person events. I think that as the world becomes increasingly AI-y, there will be a polar opposite demand for IRL. So people want to do more in-person experiences. So to the degree you have possible. So I don't care what business you're in. There is a way that you can do a client appreciation event or it can be included in the services you have. People want to be together, especially people that are local, especially people that are nationally have similar interests. It's common to want to be with other people like you. And what's crazy is that at in-person events, you can stage so many different testimonial collecting moments and show social proof in the best way possible, socially. That those become like, I used to think to myself, I ran these events with, I didn't sell anything at our delivery events. All we did was collect ads for them. So we would literally just use them once a quarter so we could just get the next 12 weeks of ads. That was it. They do it as marketing events. So if you're not doing that now, you're missing out. The next is communication, communities, and review site scrapes. Okay, so how does this work? So this is more of a process that you can install inside of your business. But, and again, why are we doing this? You're probably not making enough ads. So then how can I make it easier for you to make ads? Well, how do we make ads out of the stuff that we already do? You're already doing a life cycle with your customers. Great, let's document it. If you aren't doing some sort of in-person event or appreciation, you should, because it's the easiest way. Everyone's excited. They'll happily record stuff for you. The next is you've got the, if you have like a school community, or you have your review. So this is like, I'll give you a simple one for me. It's like, if I have a book that has 30,000 reviews on it, I can just take screenshots of all the reviews and all of those are ads. And I don't know which one's gonna work, but I'll bet you there's some that are pretty good. And so review sites, if you have TrustPilot, Yelp, Google, Facebook, any of these sites, take your reviews individually and see sometimes you have pictures that go with them, sometimes you have videos that go with those. Those make great good ads. You've probably seen, there was a great campaign by Liquid Death where they just took their one star reviews. And basically they're like, we're not for everybody. So you can take a counter approach. But all of these things, again, I'm trying to look at what are the things that I can give you that can immediately make you more ads without you having to do anything. So that's the fourth. The fifth is a testimonial competition. Strongly, strongly recommend doing this. You have to disclose it in the ads, of course, that you collected this from a competition or has some sort of incentive. It probably won't affect the results either way. But just FYI, if you're in the US for FTC. Beyond that though, testimonial competition. So give away a grand prize like you normally would of something super valuable and just tell people, your entire email list or customer base that they can all compete and the way that they can enter is by submitting an ad. And then you give them a script of what the ad should be like. An example of what your best ad, give your best ad as an example, have them make it, and you're gonna get a bunch of trash. But you're also gonna get a handful of diamonds. And you can do that once every two quarters. But you'll get a huge deluge of ads that come in that you can use. Now, next is, and by the way, how do you say who's the winner? You actually run them as ads and you tell them, the person who gets the best results is the winner. So instead of you trying to guess which ad's gonna perform, just let the algorithm do that and tell them that that's how you're judging it because then they'll make better ads. Next one is delivery or outcome moment. So if you ever have any kind of visual delivery of like a physical product or some sort of experience or some sort of transformation that someone goes through as becoming a customer of yours, like this is what unboxings look like. These are all amazing things that you can capture as moments as long as you put a process around it. So the way that I would do this is that, so I have bonuses, milestones, awards, right? What I have for that is, if someone wants to win some sort of recognition, they have to give me a testimonial in order to get the recognition, right? Or if we know that we're gonna meet with them, let's say that on check in number one, we can just have a testimonial about this milestone. So I say, hey, did you make your first high ticket sale? Yes, ah, how's it feel? Oh, did you recruit your first customer? Yes, oh, did you get your first job offer? Yes, hey, here's me delivering this custom made vault to your house, isn't this amazing? Yeah, like, you just wanna capture the moments. And so most of you guys have moments in the business that are valuable. And we just need to make sure that you put a process around capturing them. Because the whole point of this whole section here is, how do I get you guys more content for advertisements that are impressive and compelling which you're already doing, you're just not telling anyone. Now, this next one is probably one of my most used personally. For those of you who are in the organic game, like very high leverage, but organic plus CTA, all right? So this is how this works. So let's say that this is my Instagram feed, all right? So I've got, whatever, 12 tiles of Instagram posts, right? Let's say that this is my one of 12 posts, right? What I'm gonna do is I'm gonna pull this guy out and I'm gonna run, let's say that the video looks like this, right? So it's kind of like an editor, all right? Then I'm gonna put my CTA here and I'm gonna run this whole thing as an ad. So we have your generic content that we know performed. We add in our call to action related to either our lead magnet or our offer. So the point here is that you don't need to make every one of your pieces of content be some sort of advertisement because that would suck, right? Just make it valuable, make it good, have a good strong hook on it and the ones that outperform organically, like if Meta or YouTube will give you free impressions, they will happily give you paid impressions because it means that people already enjoy this thing enough that they stays on their platform. So they will display your ads more because it's not like a detractor, right? If you think about like a jab, jab, jab, right? Hook that whole concept for a platform, ads are disruptive to the platform. So they will show your ads if the ads are good and organic to more people for less money. So this is a mega, mega hack for those of you who make a lot of content. And I think what ends up happening, this is my opinion, is that over time, you're paid in your content side, just start to merge closer and closer together until it really just becomes media, right? Where you're making a ton and ton of content and the outperformance here gets stapled with CTAs and lead magnets and offers and get run and blasted over here, which means that you're literally building your brand because you know the content was good and you're getting more of the right type of people, but then you're also generating a return on it. That's how this evolves, right? Now, the last one is UGC if possible. So I'll give you two examples of this. So some of you guys might be able to get customers to work for free and you put them together who are hardcore believers in whatever it is that you sell and you put a group together and then they get some sort of quid pro quo or some sort of exchange where they make these testimonial videos on a weekly basis and you have this kind of army of people who are making content for you that you can then basically create ads out of. Like for school right now, this is one of the things that we do, right? A lot of the ads that you see is like, we have this big school community, surprise, surprise, of people who love school and just like want to make ads for school. We actually don't, I'll just leave it there, right? Like it works very, very well. This is what TikTok shop has done very well for anybody who's in consumer products. And so it's like, how can we have a TikTok shop of five kind of effect that we can build within our business? And you do that by bringing the zealots in, the people who love your product the most or love your services the most to then help you make the content to get more people in. And then I'll do this as a small side note. For those of you who are bigger businesses, right? Or realize that you need to have, like this is especially true for physical products. If you were in a super commoditized space, one of the big differentiators is obviously brand. And so if you can get some influencers who you can run ads from and you can whitelist it, as in like you run from their account but they sell to your thing, worth its weight in gold. So obviously you can have your kind of like micro influencer kind of army or you can go like how can I get two to five really strong mega or in between mega and just big influencers that I can run my ads from. So that's how I think about getting more ads. If ads is how you're getting your customers, those are all the ways that I incorporate more ads into the business. So now it's like, okay, we made more ads but how do we make better ads? So hooks are still 80% of the battle. Like it's hard for me to emphasize this too much but they're everything. And I'll give you an example for this. So two of our, so two companies that we have in Pervolia for many years spent millions and millions and millions and millions of dollars in ads. And there's basically like three or four hooks that worked for that whole period of time. And so I bring this up because like we have this assumption that we need to like create new hooks and if anything, the hook is like the hardest part to figure out for the right avatar. And then the rest of it is what you end up swapping out over and over again over the years. But like a good hook can last for a very, very long time especially as you're going to new and newer markets. They haven't heard this before and especially from you. And so we like to kaleidoscope out the process which is fundamentally like how many different permutations of this ad can we make? Can we change the color? Can we change the font? Can we change the actor? Can we change the clothing? Can we change the background? Right, can we change the speed? Can we get to 1.2? Can we get to 0.9? Like all of these things are permutations that you can make of winning ads. And that's taking the same ad and just basically cloning it. And this is what this like this one thing is we'll get you 5x, 10x more at every existing ad set. Like me, like when I realized that it changed my advertising. So many people might think it's like, oh, they've made, you know, they have all these ads that are running, it's like, yes, of course we do. But we find our winners and then we just try to make a hundred different versions of that one winner. So there's an ad that I think I have in one of the playbooks like that's Kail and Maggie in front of their gym. I probably made $10 million from that ad. But it wasn't that one ad. I made a hundred versions of that ad. We like we even remixed the ad and it still worked. Right, like when you have the right, when you have those raw pieces that are there, you wanna squeeze the living hell out of it. Now, if you're like, okay, this sounds great. How do I find these great ads? Well, I put four sets of tools there. So one of 10.com is very good for packaging and hooks. Super cheap. I'm sure they'll be very grateful that we sent them, you know, zillion customers. That's not in the affiliate link. Facebook ads library, TikTok creative center, similar web, SCM rush, A-Refs. All of those are good places that you can look for ad inspiration. I'll say that TikTok creative center and Facebook ads library are the two free ones. So you can just like go check those out for free. But this is what I do in my free time. I also save ads. Any ads that I find interesting, I save. And when I get into ad creating mode, I pull up my swipe file of all the ads that I've seen recently across any industry that I thought was interesting for whatever reason. And then when I get in kind of like my creative flow, I then go pull those up. But my process for making ads is I rewatch my top winners. I actually do this. I rewatch my top winners. And then I look at my new inspirations for the week. And then I make more ads. Now, some of you guys, and I touched on this earlier, but some of you guys are getting stuck like with your ads because you're like, I can't like, let me ask this question. Who here would spend more on your ads? But you can't because you're just not making enough. Basically, it gets too expensive. Like you're like, it works here. And then we double our spend. And all that happens is we double how much it costs us to get leads. And I get some, OK, I need you to pay super close attention to this. I like, pay attention to this. This is for you. So the five stages of awareness here, all right? This is what Eugene Schwartz defined in his seminal book, Breakthrough Advertising. Those are the five levels. You have must aware, product aware, solution aware, problem aware, unaware. So many of you, most of you, when you feel like you hit a ceiling, it's not that you hit a ceiling. You actually did saturate your little market. It's just that there's a lot more people, but not with your existing messaging. So what are these five stages? So the most aware, these are the customers who already know your product and only need to know whatever your dealer offer is. So when you do lead with an offer, go buy my thing. You will make money. It's just going to be the highest return on the smallest number of people. Then you're going to move up to product aware people. The customer knows what you sell, but isn't sure if it's right for them yet. Then you've got solution aware people. The customers know the result they want, but they don't know that your product can provide it. They know they need more leads, but they don't know if SEO is the way or posting more organic content, or running paid ads, or doing outreach. They just know they need more leads. Problem aware, customers sense they have a problem, but they don't know there is a solution to it. And then you've got somebody who's completely in a work. Customer doesn't know they have a problem or need. All right? And so if you think about this, it's kind of like a reverse watermelon. You'll have your most concentrated sales per eyeballs, but down here. Now, of course, the money model needs to evolve so that as you go to these colder and colder audiences, you can still make money, because it'll cost you more to acquire customers as you go into each of these kind of break into these new segments of the audience. And so I want to give you these examples here, because I think they're super valuable. But most aware, for many of you guys, your hooks are typically offer-driven. 50% off are best-selling XYZ thing for the rest of the month. And so the explanation is the hook targets customers who already know the business and its products or services. It focuses on a specific avatar with a clear incentive. And the goal is to get people who are on the fence to take action. That's the point. But that only works for this many people. And so please hear me when I'm saying this. If you keep hitting this wall for your ads, it's because you're only targeting your most aware or sometimes product-aware people. You're not getting into solution aware. You're not getting into problem aware. Now, instead of me reading these, I'll just break down. Most aware is that you have ads that are offer-driven. Product-aware, typically you can have proof. And that'll do it. Solution-aware, they'll typically be led with promise. Problem-aware will be more pain. And completely unaware is going to be driven off curiosity. All right? So I'll give you a kind of example. So like first one, 50% off for the rest of the month, that's a deal. That's an offer. Proof-driven, discover why 10,000 people chose XYZ to solve the problem this month. Solution-aware, promise-driven. The fastest way to achieve, fastest way to build a business online, school. Hey, are you tired of trying to figure out a way to make your first dollar online? There's a better way than XYZ. So if you ever see like, you're trying to hear like crypto and trading and all these things. It's like, oh, OK, so these are maybe some things. There's a different way. There's a better way. And then you have curiosity-driven. The hidden danger in your daily routine that's costing you. Curiosity. So this would be an example for a B2C product. So XFAST, new formula, 25% more protein, same grade taste. Proctaware, why not 9 out of 10 XFAST users reach their goal weight within three months? Solution-aware, lose 15 pounds in 30 days with our proven replacement system. Problem-aware, frustrated with crash dice that don't last. There's a sustainable way to shed pounds. Completely unaware, the hidden hormonal imbalance that's making one in three Americans gain weight. So if you've seen those kind of crazy ads on tabula or on the side panels that say that, it's because they're going for an ocean of traffic. They're going for the absolute most-aware people. But that ad, if they can get the money to work, will scale for a very long time. Because there's so many more people up here than down here. And so you keep hitting these walls because all of your hooks are targeted for a very specific avatar. Or somebody's in a very specific part of the buying cycle. And so if you change your hooks, you'll change the targeting. You'll change who gets pulled in. And so these are a bunch of B2B examples, but it works the same way. So most-aware, social media management, 20 cent first month free, whatever. And then completely unaware, unexpected way your business is losing thousands each month because you don't have ads. So with that being said, how do we make better creative? I have this very simple process, which I outlined on this picture here. But this is how I actually do it. I write out 50 plus hooks. And we do three to five meets. I literally did this last week for new workshop ads that went out yesterday. This is how we actually do it. And I think people somehow think that there's some magic bullet to this. But I've just been following this process for seven years, eight years. I don't even know. Many years. When I had my gyms, I just did them all together. But once I got into gym, I learned how to advertise a little better. It's just like separate the hooks from the meat and the CTAs. What we have found is that it doesn't affect. So I used to think, oh, I want to do it in one take. It'll be better. Zero effect whatsoever on the output, which means that you can absolutely assemble the ads rather than try to write them. It just makes your life 100 times easier. Just think of great hooks. Think of your three to five really amazingly valuable things or belief-breaking concepts. And then just make a call to action. That's it. What's cool here is that you can get just from this little combination 150 to 750 ads a week. So for those who are like, man, how am I going to get that kind of volume? It's like volume should never be your concern. Volume should not be your concern. It should not be your worry. If anything, the issue here is you'll test all these are messaging angles, and then you want to have different visual hooks, different background effects, different actors, all of those things. Once you have the messaging right, messaging rarely needs to get changed. And this is something that I didn't understand. It's like, what's our new angle? It's like, you don't need a new angle. The angle you have works. Just change the spokesperson, change the background, change the color, change the pacing, change the fonts. Like change everything. Once you have the messaging that works, it's about changing everything else. So those are just some of the quick and dirty ways that I think they're making better creative. Now, the next is going to be targeting. So it's about getting the right eyes to see it. So I had a chat, chat jibba da, make this little ad for us here, like 50% off bathing suits, and it's like the middle of winter. Well, it doesn't matter how good the ad is or how good the offer is. If you're in the middle of winter, that ad's not going to convert. If you're making ads, you know, you're in Iowa and your ads get served in Florida, you're not going to do very well. You give the best to everything. And so one of the common sayings of the marketing world is that the list matters more than anything, even more than your offer. Like, I'll put this as an extreme. If I had just the absolute highest buyers, right, and I said, hey, here's an offer. Like, it's super expensive and very exclusive, and five of you can do it, you don't really need to do a lot of selling because you already have such a good list. That's the horror part is getting that list together. On the flip side, if I had that exact same thing and went to a zillion people and they didn't know who I was or it wasn't relevant to them, it's not going to work. So targeting is important. So this is my kind of troubleshooting process for creating better targeting. So the first level of this is like, are the right types of people opting in? And so one of the things that I do, especially if you're just getting into ads or getting into a new targeting option, is that I do deep types on the opt-in. So what that means is I'll look at the opt-ins, I'll look up their Google account, I'll look up other accounts that are associated with it, look at who they are, and be like, are these the types of people that I want? Am I looking for chiropractors? Great, are they chiropractors? OK, great. If they're not, then are they physical therapists? Am I getting close? Are they doctors? If no, I need to change the targeting. If sort of, then the process I'm going to outline can help fix it. And if yes, these you do improve the other stuff to improve the funnel, but not your targeting. So once you have the targeting, it's like, great, don't mess with that. Like, the targeting is good. Now we just need to make the money model work when you do the friction in the funnel, when you make the opt-in better, make the offer better, make the headline clear, all that kind of stuff. Now, I will give you a little example that I do at our workshops here, but I think people will get a lot of value from it, so I will do it for you guys here. As soon as I have a sip of agua. OK, so let's see here. John Archibald Smith. I want to ask you a question here. If I said for $100,000, I will double your business by getting a whole separate channel of advertising paid ads to work. Would you say yes? So whatever you're doing, $100,000, we double your advertising. OK, great. Now, this is what happens in reality most times. So we say yes, of course, I would take $100,000 to double the business. Fine. But let's say a month and one, I spend $10,000, and then nothing happens. But we spend $10,000, and we just figure out that we got some clicks that went to the page, and those clicks, some people opted in, but they weren't necessarily right people. But we ran some ads. So month two, we dropped 10 grand, and this time we clarify the headline, clarify the offer. We get the opt-ins, and we're like, OK, they're all chiropractors, we think, but they're not scheduling. So then, month three, we spend $10,000 again. We already have the opt-ins, and we have the headlines on us. But then this time, we clarify the reason to schedule a call. We tie it to the benefit of the lead magnet. We make sure the process auto fills out. Their name and phone number and email, so it's a one-click schedule right there. And then we're like, awesome. So now we're getting bookings. But now, no one's showing up, so we're like, shit. So then the next, so after we spent $10,000 three times, no, we haven't made money yet. So we dropped $10,000. We run water through the funnel again. This time, we say, all right, well, let's add more friction in. And so before they can book the call, they got to watch a video, and they got to say that they're the right type of person, and that they're excited to do this thing. Then we put a 60-second delay to make sure they start watching the video. Then they can schedule the call. Then all of a sudden, we're getting calls booked, and they're starting to show. Now, the way that this will typically happen is that most business owners can logically say, oh, if I spent $100,000 this year, and it doubled, I got a second revenue line that decreased my risk, because now I have two different ways of getting customers. And I made twice as much revenue with less incremental cost, which would mean probably more than a double profit. That would be an amazing move for the business. It'd be a bannery year for the business. Great. But in reality, is they spend $10,000, or $20,000, or $30,000, and they say, I spent $30,000, and I didn't get anything. It's like, well, duh. Because the thing is, is that you will make no money until you finally get the first dollar across the bridge. And so what will happen is you'll spend 10, make nothing, spend 10, make nothing, spend 10, make nothing, spend 10, make two, and then on that fifth month, you'll spend 10. And then you'll have the leads that come in, they'll opt in, they'll watch the video, they'll schedule, and the sales team will be prepped, and you make 50. And just like that, you have gotten back everything that you lost. But it'll take time. And I bring this up because what I would encourage you to think about is that marketing always works. It's just a matter of efficiency. Advertising always works. It's just about how efficient you are. That's it. And so instead of saying, those don't work for me, just say, I don't know how to make them efficient enough for my business. And then it becomes something that you can control. So we tweak each of these aspects of the funnel until we finally get the throughput we want. But what I would implore you to do is just focus on making progress. Just focus on improving the one constraint that's in front of you and instead of saying meta ads don't work. You have to think, did we get clicks? Yes, we got clicks. OK, are they opting in? No, go back to the ad, clarify the ask, make sure the target is right. Are we getting opt-ins? Yes, what do we need next? We need them to book. Great, they're not booking. Go back, let's go through the click journey again. Just focus on the one thing that's in front of you and just solve them one at a time. Rather than just think, this doesn't work. I've built a lot of acquisition channels across a lot of different types of platform and industries and mediums. It stops being this mythology. You just have to make it clear and obvious that this is what they need to do. That's it. Now, let's say that you're going to get into a new channel. This is my targeting cheat sheet. So if you have no way of targeting, the easiest way to start is just interest groups, demographics, and geographies. What areas the country or what areas the world, what things are these types of people interested in? Are they a certain age? Whatever. We start there. Once we get a certain amount of opt-ins, then we'll do a look-alike off of the opt-ins, 1, 2, 3%. Once we get a certain amount of calls, then that's better than an opt-in. Then we'd have 1, 2, 3% look-alikes. And again, if I'm saying opt-ins and calls, and you have a different sales motion, it could be opt-ins and webinar registrations. Like, all this flows the same way. It's more just thinking about step 1, step 2, step 3 in the funnel process. That's all this is. Step 4 is look-alike off of sales. And then you would have a look-alike off the highest value customers. And once you have that pixel, and the reason that it's so difficult to get there is that you need 1,000 fires. And so getting 1,000 opt-ins is easier than getting 1,000 calls, which is easier than getting 1,000 sales, which is easier than getting 1,000 upsells. But once you have 1,000 upsells, that's a very high-quality customer base. And then what happens is that over time, that data, that pixel, actually becomes almost like a competitive advantage for you as a business. And so then you can start opening up your targeting overall with a pixel-thirty pre-trained on your best customer. Kind of neat. And so just keep making progress. And over time, that pixel will become a competitive advantage. And so just here's a few rules of thumb that I have. I just walked through kind of like building out this hypothetical funnel with you. I almost always make more money increasing friction and increasing cost per action. So I care far more about ROAS than cost per lead. And so I'll give you a common example here. So let's say that you've got $100 CPL and a $500 lifetime gross profit. 5 to 1, not bad. Or would you rather have a $500 CPL cost per lead to get a $5,000 lifetime gross profit? 10 to 1. Well, almost everybody here would say, well, I'd rather get 10 to 1 than 5 to 1. But you might not act that way. Because if I actually 5x your lead cost, you might be like, whoa, slow down, bro. Like why don't you just 5x my lead cost? This is crazy. This is huge. But you have to change how you think in terms of orders of magnitude. Because if you're trying to get these $100 leads to turn into million-dollar customers, it is a waste of time. But what's interesting is that the air up market is almost always thinner. Sorry, it's less populated. Because so few people think this way. It's a less constrained way of thinking. People always think about, how do I sell to whoever's in front of me? Rather than being able to say, no, I'm not going to sell to you, I'm going to be willing to spend five times more to get a customer. Because I know that this customer is worth 10 times more to my business. That's what people don't have the discipline to do because they can't say no to money. I think I have a good example for this coming up in a section too. Just kind of neat. I think you'll like. Now, once we have better targeting, so at this point, it's like we fixed our offer. We fixed our lead magnet. We installed these systems to get more ads into the business. We have better creative using more hooks that can get into new segments of the marketplace. We have better targeting that we walked through that process for. Now we can go get cheaper clicks. So I said cheaper clicks and I corrected here to higher return because I think, again, I want to break this belief for as many of you as possible. It is technically correct that you could have a cheaper ad platform. But it's rarely the answer. It's more so. It's more typically like we just want the platform to get the most back from. And so this is when you need to expand channels once you're already crushing it. Not when you can't make something work. So I want to make this point because I want to emphasize it. Some people are like, hey, I can't make my channel work. So I'm going to start on another channel. That is very rarely the correct answer. You almost always make the thing that you're doing work well or rather make it work so well that you can then say, OK, now I'm going to take this. And then I'm like, I only expand on your channels once I have a channel that works. So go make the channel you're currently on. Keep tweaking until it works. And then you can scale across to the channels. And I'll say this because fundamentally, as much as like, yeah, business professionals are being linked in. And like TikTok is for more consumer stuff. It's like, sure, those are trends and those are true. But fundamentally, most people are on most platforms. So fun one on here. Can you in the chat just like IG, TT, YT for YouTube, X, AMZ? If it's a, well, I didn't even say what I was going to. I was going to say, where did you find me first? Or either where'd you find me first? Or where did you consume most of my stuff? OK, so a lot of YouTube, a lot of Instagram. A lot of YouTube, a lot of Instagram. Yeah, I'm looking. Some pod? Ah, pod. OK, good to know. Some pod? Yeah. And so you can see that people are all over the place. People are all over the place. And so sure you're going to get access to new crowds. But fundamentally, there's enough customers on almost every platform. That's my point. And so I'll give you a little bit of a tool, a little bit more advanced thing here. But to further reinforce the point that I made about bidding for the right objectives. So it's common to have a campaign that's like a cost-per-lead campaign versus a row-ass campaign or return on ad spend. Now, this is typically only available to people who do some sort of transactions. But if there's a way you can do a transaction, highly encourage it. Now, the reason for this is I want to give you a real-world example. So for our book launch, CPL campaigns drove leads at about a third of the cost. So a third of the cost leads. So for every dollar, we get three times the leads if we did a CPL campaign versus a row-ass campaign. But look at this data. This is nuts. So campaigns that drove leads got around $5 leads, roughly. And the row-ass from those leads-driven campaigns was 4x, meaning those $5 leads were worth $20. Here's where it gets kind of nutty. The lead costs on our row-ass-driven campaigns was closer to $17, so almost more than three times the price. But our row-ass on those campaigns was $11. So that means that those $17 leads were worth $187, whereas the $5 leads were worth $20. It's a more than 9x relative difference. The reason I want to show this to you is because when I made the point earlier about $100 lead versus a $500 lead being worth, in that example, I said twice as much. But this is real math simply based on targeting. And so I'm bringing this up for you because there are segments of the marketplace that absolutely can afford what you want and that you can deliver a superior service to you because they're the right customer. They just probably cost more on some of these initial actions, but they're still worth so much more. And so instead of saying, like, I've got these couple customers that I love and most of my customers suck, try and build the business not to be the middle point of what these brokies can barely afford. And what the rich people wildly are undervaluing because they're like, this guy's so cheap, right? They would happily pay you five times more because they're the right customer. And so the thing is that you have to adjust. This is why it's so difficult for me to separate leads from sales, from pricing, because a lot of this stuff does work together. But you have to get the right avatar. And then you have to price it accordingly. And so a lot of you guys get stuck in this no man's land where it's like you're pricing like you're selling to brokies, but you want to sell to richies, but richies don't believe you because you're pricing like you're selling to a broky. So don't do that. It has to be congruent all the way across, which means you have to be willing to say no. So this is just quick and dirty. If I had to pick platforms that I'd be focused on if you are selling purely to consumers, I'd be mastering Meta, YouTube and TikTok. There's kind of my 80, 20. If you're B2B, then it'd be Meta, YouTube and then LinkedIn and X. Those would be my, like if I had to be one or the other, like if I had to just pick three, it's what I do. Obviously I'd prefer you just do all of them. It's what I do. But like if you have like more constrained resources, I would think about it like that. Now, actually I think, no, I think I saved this for later. Okay, so the last in our little ad section for you guys who are running ads, okay? Building brand through content. Instead of talking about that, because if you want your ads to actually perform, having a big brand will make all of your ads do better. And so, but me saying, hey, build a brand is you're probably not gonna do it through paid ads. You're gonna do it through other means, which is gonna be organic, which then kind of opens us up for organic. So that kind of concludes our mini section on ads in terms of for those of you who are like, okay, I am not getting the leads at the price that I want. I'll do it. I probably could do a better review here, but fundamentally, number one, if you're not getting as many as you want from ads, look at your lead magnator offer first and be the highest leverage thing that you can do. Number one, number two, you're gonna leverage as much as you possibly can within your existing business to create more ads. You're gonna have testimonial campaigns. You're gonna have unboxing videos. You're gonna scrape the review sites. You're going to have in-person events. You're gonna have life cycle ads. All of these things are things that your business is already doing that you just need to harness and document publicly. Then from a better creative perspective, it's like, can we change the hooks to match the customer segments? Can we do broader hooks so we can break into these new markets? From there, we think about targeting itself. Is there a way that we can first start with interest and demographics and then eventually work our way up the look-alike ladder until eventually we're at the right types of customers? But this is so important because most people then think, oh, I think I need cheaper ad platforms when really you wanna be bidding for the highest return prospects on the highest return platforms, which means that maybe you should be willing to pay three times more for the lead if it's worth 10 times as much. And so looking at your actual customer base and saying, who are these people? Where do these people live? What do these people want lead magnet offer? What kind of creative do these people respond to? What's the background? Who's the spokesperson? These are the things that are kind of more strategic in nature that will ultimately unlock the effectiveness of the ads. Now, of course, all of these things can be proven if you have a banger brand, which, like if Taylor Swift gets in an ad, it doesn't matter what she's promoting, it'll probably do really well because she has a great brand. She could have a terrible offer, she could have only one ad, she have dog shit creative, she could have probably, well, for her targeting it doesn't matter, because most people know who she is. And she can build whatever platform she wants. And so everything, the first five things are things that matter if you don't have a brand. If you have a brand, it makes all these things just way more powerful, which then brings us to organic overall. And so we already talked about the better offer slash email, so I'm not gonna hit that. Okay, a lot of Instagram, a lot of YouTube, LinkedIn, Instagram, LinkedIn, SEO. Okay, cool, good mix. Little SEO, little OnlyFans. Cool, I got you Brady. I see you bro. Okay, let's move on to making more content then. So here's the big myth, all right? The big myth is that you can max out platforms. So who here, you probably already heard this, but like you can't make more than two pieces of down Instagram, right? You can't make more than, you know, one YouTube video a week. Mr. Beast makes a YouTube video once a week, so therefore that's the best thing. The answer is no. And it's kind of like the original thing with like, I think I've saturated my market. There are so many eyeballs in these platforms. There's no way that you've saturated the eyeballs of the platform. Like, it's just, it's such a like a, we're at the center of the universe perspective, which is how humans are. And so it's normal to have this incredibly self-centered bias that like you are so important, that everyone is seeing your thing. It's like the reality that 1% of your audience who follows you actually even sees your post is probably like crazy to you. And so if you post three times, maybe 1%, three different 1% actually see your stuff. You're still so far below the amount of people who are actually gonna get tired from your stuff. And the thing is, is these algorithms are very, very good. And so here's what they'll do. They just won't show your second post to the same people. They'll show it to other people. And if they don't show it to those people in your audience, they'll show it to new people. And so there's a Bollywood account right now on Instagram that can see billion views a month and it's posting a hundred clips per day. There is no limit. Moseri, who's the head of Instagram, answers questions like this, there's no limit. It's just the reality is you only max out the number of good posts you can make. Like you might just not be able to, you physically or given the resources you have or the skills you have, you might not be able to make 10 great posts on Instagram a day. That might not be in the cards for you. But you might be able to only make two good posts a day, which is why you then believe the mythology that you can't post more than two times. And so once you draw that line in the sand, you say, oh, that's it, there's no way. But that's just not true. I promise you it's not true. And this applies for YouTube, it applies for TikTok, it applies for everything. And what's interesting is that people have like a main platform and then sometimes the platform they like quote, don't care about, they'll put a ton of volume on. And they're like, oh yeah, that's like my test platform. They're all your test platform. They all work like this. They want you to put as much content as humanly possible because they have the eyeballs. So again, this is a bit of a belief breaker here. But for YouTube, I post three shorts a day and I do one or two longs a week. That's just on YouTube. Okay, just YouTube. Layla posts three shorts a day and one or two longs a week. Just YouTube. Dave Ramsey, he has nine channels. Just on his two main channels does four shorts a day and three longs per day. Three longs per day. Do you want to know why Dave Ramsey is bigger than you? Because he does more than you. He's been doing it longer. PBD, some guys might have heard, Patrick McDavid. He's got a political thing. Four shorts a day, nine longs a day. One channel, nine longs a day. Nine. And you're like, man, it's not fair that these guys have bigger brands than me. They do more than you. And we do it for a long time. And we look at what we did and we see ask, how can we get better? And I'll say that this is me shooting from the hip here, but I would say that most of like, I'd say like taste on platforms changes every like, I want to say like six months or so, especially for the shorts, less so for the longs, but like short form platforms like TikTok, Instagram, like especially those two, it's like things move in and out of vogue about every six months. And so if you have like a format that's crushing it, it's like you ride it for how long you can and then you have to just change the format up. It's not like the message changes, just the packaging. But that packaging is like what makes it feel native to the platform and then ultimately perform better. And so you have to just like be in touch with that. And like this is skill, like you'll start recognizing it. And you'd be like, oh, we should do something like that in our industry and then do it. The first consideration is that you should just make more content, make more content, orders of magnitude, more content, you're like, I post once a day on Instagram, that's how I make content. It's like, no shit. I make like a hundred times more content than you, literally a hundred times. That's why I have more. And you know what? I probably have lower returns than you do per piece of content. But I still have higher returns overall. And I'm not an optimizer. Optimizers try to get the most for the least. I'm a maximizer. I just want the most. Maximizers win by the way. So the next one is better CTA. So if you have organic, the first thing we fix is, can we fix the lead magnet or offer? Which I already covered. And so both of those basically just lead you to your business owner or you wanna start a business, right? So it's either school or it's roadmap. So the reason this is important though, is that you wanna make sure it's above the fold so that the links and the CTAs are there. And one of the cool things about YouTube, which a lot of people don't know this by the way, is that you can gross change all YouTube subscriptions across all videos at once. If you don't know how to do it, Google it, all right? But the reason this is so valuable for me is that, basically, I see every piece of content I've ever made on YouTube as a floating ad. It's like my channel collectively gets 30 million impressions a month, or whatever it is, 30 million views a month. And my whole goal is to say, where do I want these 30 million views to go? It's I put in this regular work every single month for those 30 million views to occur. You just want your best performing value post of all time. I think this is intelligently, this is why I did it, because it's already tested, people like it, they're more likely to share it, enjoy it, and get value from it. So I just have like my CTA, and then my highest performing post of all time is underneath. Now, the third place for CTAs inside of YouTube is gonna be the one third. So you can look at my videos almost like clockwork, one third into the video is where we do our CTAs. The reason there is because ideally, you'd wait till the absolute end, and you'd have the hottest prospects, you would. But you're gonna miss out on like 80% of your traffic, because the vast majority of your traffic is gonna be there in the beginning and not in the end. It's just the nature of YouTube. All right, and so I want to not ask right in the first few seconds, because I haven't provided value yet, but at the one third point, I'm pretty okay with it. You'll also notice that in my books, my review ask is not at the halfway point, it's at the one third point. Basically, after the first main meaty section is where I ask for it. It's the same concept. It's like I just wanna make sure I provided enough value, and they say, hey, by the way, can you leave review? By the way, go check out a scaling roadmap. Now, in terms of how do you make these? And I know a lot of people are very afraid, and so I wanna break this belief again. For those of you who make YouTube videos, some people are like, I never wanna ask for anything in my videos. You can do whatever you want. I will say that I don't think it matters, and I'll explain why. I don't think it matters because it's always a ratio. If I have a 60 second ad two times in a 57 minute video, I'm 30 to one on my give to ask ratio. I'm fine. And if you think about the big, take it to the hypothetical stream. Look at Rogan. Rogan is one of the biggest platforms ever. He's got like eight minutes of ads in his stuff. Some people skip the ads, some people don't care. Many of you guys have like link trees. I would strongly recommend against link trees. Pick the thing you want them to do. One front door, don't give someone another choice. Just like, you know how hard it is to get someone to just decide to click the link? Don't give them a link and then more shit. Just like commit. Pick whatever the front door of the business is, or the thing you wanna promote, promote it. All right? You will, I'm telling you, you will be really happy about it. B2B, this is just a little bit of a hack for you, by the way. If you are B2B, and you're like, I've got this big TikTok and it's just not doing very well for me. I learned this from a different business owner who had turned his TikTok into something that actually worked for high ticket. So here's the hack. I'm gonna give you the juice. Have your CTA on TikTok, be DM me on Instagram. I know when saying that sounds. But for whatever reason, Instagram is just a better conversion platform. And when people see you there for whatever reason, it has just, it's a different vibe. I don't know. People are just willing to spend way more money on Instagram. I don't know why, I'm just telling you what. So instead of the link being, you know, whatever your thing is, the link would be a link to either directly DM you or to just your Instagram straight up. Don't ask me why it works. I'm just saying if you're doing B2B high ticket, you probably haven't made TikTok work that well because very few people have. If you're doing B2B high ticket, there's what it do. If you're doing low ticket stuff, TikToks, you already know how to make this work. All right, for LinkedIn, the big hack with LinkedIn is all around comments. So I have actually have a very large LinkedIn following. And we do pretty well on LinkedIn. And the big thing is it's all about comments. And so you want to get as many people as possible to comment because comments are like the algorithm there. And so if I come in with someone's stuff, my whole audience sees me, sees my comment on, and it doesn't matter the size of the post. So like I can go, this happens all the time. Like I'll find somebody who says like, hey, I read Alex's book and changed my life or whatever. It has like two likes on it. And then I'll say, thanks dude, like all you, whatever. And then all of a sudden that thing will have 1200 likes. And so for an account that has like usually like one to five likes on average, it's because the way the LinkedIn algorithm works is that all of my audience will see that one post because they'll see my comment. So super, super skew towards obviously valuable posts, but making sure that it's something that it, I don't want to say incites comments, but you want to give some good reason to comment for people. Also pinning the comment in your comments with your CTA. So if you have like a banger post, then you'll say, hey, by the way, here's my thing. Typically for most of the platforms, if you have a link out off of platform, it will underperform the post. So it'll push down the impressions. And so just putting, just pinning the comment as first comment, especially if you have over time, they'll start to like, your comments will typically get liked more than everyone else's. It'll just kind of get like double viewed. So not only will it get viewed because it was the top comment in general, but because other people liked it too, and that brings their audience as well. All right, so that's the hack for LinkedIn in terms of turning CTAs. For X, this, I mean, I spent a lot of time on X, and so I'll just tell you some of the quick and dirty ones. Shopify integration for lead magnet. So see the Mosey Money Minute right there, the newsletter. You can basically get a $0 product on Shopify and integrate it with X. And so then people can basically hit that. So you have this big ass banner on your profile for people to just opt in for stuff. This generates like another, I wanna say like a hundred leads a day for me, just from me making that one integration. Doesn't sound like a huge amount, but like, it's not nothing. And again, all of these are kind of like lines, lines in the water that are just bringing people in. Now, if you make a post on X, this is where threads or single posts, the best way that I've seen work with X for those of you who are on X is like thread of like 10 things, first three or four things, then you put your CTA, then three or four more things, then you put your final CTA. So two CTAs embedded in the post as part of the thread works really well. And if you have a single post, you can have a comment CTA basically on every post if you want to. I think if you do it too much, it feels a little weird, but if you do it every once in a while, I think it works great. Now, let's talk about email, because I think this is super important and it sounds like a very boring thing, but some of you guys are like, well, I need more leads, but you really just want to make more money and you want to make ideally high profit money. And email is the single highest return platform that exists period, full stop, call me later, you have no chance, I'm right, you're wrong, like this is the highest return platform. So, and I say this so violently because of how angry I am at myself for waiting, like I'm a writer and I didn't send emails until like two years ago, and I've been doing business for a while. And it's because I hate money and I'm an idiot, that's why. So I would encourage you to not hate money and not be an idiot. So, I'm gonna go back to the beginning of this video. Oh, we're back, we are so back, we're so back. Yeah, I don't know how it turned off. Well, it did though. We'll keep it, we'll keep it front. Thanks, dudes. And we're back. Okay, so last thing was, thank you, bye for that food. Last thing was hate money and not be an idiot. Ha ha ha ha. Good, on brand. Okay, so I said 36 million emails last month, and so I learned a couple cool things. You guys are awesome. And so what I've learned about email is that, number one, it is by far the highest return platform. Number two is all, don't think about email, and this is why I think I got so intimidated by it, was like, one, I really care a lot about my writing, which is why I put it out for so long. Cause if it's gonna go out, like by the way, if you guys read anything, it comes out from acquisition.com, like I wrote it, which is crazy to say. Like, I control the written word. Cause I'm probably anal and I probably need to, need to let go of it, but I enjoy writing and I'm good at it, and so I still do it. Anyways, break email up into constituent parts, it'll make it way easier. So think about it like assembling an email, rather than writing one, and it'll just dramatically decrease the amount of stress that you have, all right? And so, because of this, and let me incentive you real quick in terms of why this is important, 25% of the revenue of acquisition.com comes from emails. Like, I mean, it's like, that's money, right? It's like millions and millions and millions and millions of dollars, a lot. So I feel kind of confident talking about it. So these are the big five, okay? I try to make this as simple as possible. Number one, subject headlines, okay? So I thought there was a lot of magic in this, and the real magic is make your emails really fucking good. And if you do that, then your emails will be a brand. So Mosey Minute is a brand. I try to make every single one of, it's very clear that it's a Mosey Minute, because if you liked my last Mosey Minute, then you will like my next one. And so, let the brand be the thing that gets you the open rate. That is my advice. Instead of trying to hack, you forgot something, or like RE, colon, as though they're responding to something, like all these little tricks and hacks, whatever. The long-term thing that only matters is that are the emails good? And so I made the decision that I wanted to not obsess about anything, besides making the brand of my emails strong. And if I do that, then people will be rewarded for opening this specific type of emails, and it could say XZ17WW, and it wouldn't matter, as long as those words were paired with good stuff. Be afraid of the time they don't copy your ideas. Because it means your ideas suck. Like honest, like I really mean this. I don't even think about it. I still appear on podcasts with them. Right. Like it just doesn't matter. It doesn't matter. No one cares. I'm not going to be 80, but I can't believe she stole my tweet. Who gives a fuck? Who fucking cares? So just give it all away. All right. So shorts versus longs, written versus video, live stream versus everything. Couple things. Making better content in terms of shorts versus longs. How much should you do of either of these? Shorts, okay. Here's how I think about this. Shorts are typically not going to be the thing that builds your brand. All right. Now you'll get awareness from this. It's better than nothing. But the objective of a short in my world is to get someone to watch along. That is the idea. Big picture though, if you can get enough quote value in a short, meaning you tell someone to do something that they can do it and something good happens, then that is valuable enough. And the goal is that if you can do that, you will get a more likely person who will respond to your request in the future. Now, second piece here, written versus video. To me, written word is the base of everything. And if I, the reason I start with written is that if something converts written only, just text, then it converts into another format. Because every other format adds two words, but words are the core. You can have a video that has no words at all and goes viral. Very hard to repurpose that into another platform. Whereas if I have a quote that will go super, super, super viral, it's going to work everywhere. Which is why I start with the most basic form that's the hardest to make go viral. So that when I put in other formats, it translates. Third thing, live streaming versus everything. So I had a conversation with Mr. Beast, I want to say like two weeks ago, right before the launch, or three weeks ago, something like that. And we were talking about content in general, and I, whatever, it doesn't matter. Here's the point. He brought this up, and it's something that I've been thinking a lot about, which is, I'll tell you the story, because I think it's worth illustrating it. So there was this big YouTubers or like, celebrities soccer match that happened. And they do it like every year, and things like UK versus US. And they have this moment during the football game or whatever that they introduce each of the players or the celebrities that influencers want at a time. And so kind of like the default, you know, fallacy measuring contest is that when your name gets called, the size of the applause is kind of like how important or influential you are, right? And so it was interesting. What they realized was that like the A-list celebrities went out and it was silent. No one cared. Then you had the shorts creators, and then they would get like a light amount of applause, like very light. Then you'd have the longs creators, the YouTubers, right? Maybe some podcasters, and you'd have more applause. And then the live streamers came out, and the whole place erupted. And so that visual story, and then the conversation they had with him where he said that one of his big grits is that he was like, I didn't go hard enough on live streaming, that's what we're going to be doing. That's what we're going to be doing at acquisition.com. So for me, I wanted to get through the launch of the book. But if you're like, hey, how do I get ahead of media? Let me tell you what I'm doing. My next 12 months is 100% focused on two words, interactivity and live. That's it. Those are the two words that will define my media strategy for the next 12 months. I want to be interactive, and I want to be live. So right now, I'm doing one of these two things. And when I take calls later, we'll have both. It'll be live and interactive. And I just, I so hardcore believe in this, because you think about like quote, time on brand. You think about reinforcement cycles with an audience. Live gives you so much more of that. And I think part of it is like there's no faking anything. Like, you guys saw my audio cut out, right? Like that's what it is, right? Like there's no filter. It's like, this is me, you know? Like there's, that's it. And so I think that it's like, if you want to have trust with an audience, then having as few filters as possible, said differently, the closest approximation to being IRL or in real life is the best way to gain affinity with the audience. And so because of technology and how things improved, that is the direction that I'm going to be going in. So do with that what you will. Beyond that, in terms of making better content, you want as much content as possible to be proof driven or demonstration driven. And that's why I also see live streaming in that same way. It's just like, it's proof. It's just living proof. Now, I talked about this on a recent podcast. I don't remember which one it was. But I'll tell you the four things that you're trying to create for yourself. So if this is for anybody who wants to make personal content, all right? So this is for you. So you have status, power, credibility, and likeness. Status is you control a scarce resource. You have something they want. Now, all four of these things increase someone's influence. If you have all four, you're extremely influential. If you have one of them, you'll have more influence than someone who has none. So a trust fund kid who just inherited their money would have more status, sorry, more influence, than someone who has no money. It's true. But would a trust fund kid who has money have more influence than someone who has no money? Influence than someone who had money and also gave you a stock tip four or five times in a row that all worked out? If you had both of them in front of you, one who had it and one who had, you certainly would believe the one who had more because they had demonstrated another element of influence, which is power, which is say-do correspondence. You give someone instructions to follow. They get a good result. They're more likely to comply with the future requests. If the future request goes well, they're more likely to continue to comply. The reason your parents still have so much power over you is because during your formative years, they gave you thousands and thousands of instructions that worked out. You might hate them or you might love them or you might not care about. It doesn't matter. The point is that it's so many cycles of reinforcing with them that they have influence on you. So I'll give you a Sheberman example. So I don't actually listen to Heberman stuff, but I saw one short clip that showed up on my newsfeed and it was about how to reverse hiccups. And I'm not somebody who really struggles with hiccups. I probably had hiccups one time this year. And I remembered watching that clip when my hiccups came and I'd watched that clip like a month earlier and the clip said how to get rid of hiccups. So I did the thing the clip said and my hiccups went away. And I was like, this is awesome. And so I'm more likely to listen to one of these little Heberman hacks in the future. He gained power over me because he gave instructions. I followed the instructions and a good thing happened. The third is credibility. If you're like, how is this related to content? Think about how you demonstrate this in content. I said, hey, send some emails. Set it in this way. If you send the emails in that way and a good thing happens, more likely you comply with a future request. The reason that I put so many steps in this stuff is for, well, it's for one, I want you to actually, we, our outcomes are aligned. If you get good results, it works for both of us, right? Like you make more and then I will make more. We will all make more. So I, that's why I spend so much time trying to bake things into like, what do we do? Right? Not what sounds nice. What do we do to get what we want to have happened to happen? So status, you demonstrate that you have stuff that people want. Number one, I have this big building. A lot of entrepreneurs want a big building. So I demonstrate that I have a big building. I get status from that. Power, I give instructions. People follow the instructions, good things happen. The third is credibility. This is proof. So I show, hey, I sold my company, $46 million. I have credibility that can build stuff. Look, my current business, look at, you know, we did a $100 million launch on Wiengid. Great, credibility. I have things that happened that prove that what I'm saying is true, right? This is where awards, medals, you know, if you said, hey, I spent $5 million in that, I said it before. I said, I sent 36 million emails, right? Now, if I had shown a screenshot of that, would have been better, but hopefully at this point, you believe me, we said 36 million emails, right? And some of you guys are like, I got all 36 million of them, right? The next one was likeness, right? Which is visual likeness, values likeness, similar to the avatar, right? Which is, I'll tell you a fun story about this. So I had, we had an event here, and I had a guy come up to me, and he said, hey man, do you mind if I get a picture? And I was like, sure, you know, whatever. And so what ended up happening, I said, by the way, you have a hardcore following in the lumberjack niche. So he was in a, he was a logger, like he cut trees down, like that was his business. And I thought it was so funny, he was like, dude, it's hardcore mosey nation. And when he told me that, I thought it was like, like the best thing ever, but it was so funny is that like, I imagine that usually me with my flannel on top, and beard and tank top, I probably look like a lot of these lumberjacks. And so for me to have a hardcore contingency of people in the lumberjack and logging community, is super believable, right? And so the point of all of this is that when we're making content, these are the four things that we're trying to accomplish. We want to demonstrate that we have the things that people want. We want to give them instructions on how to get the good things that they want. We want to prove that we have credibility third party, that the things that we have said, that demonstrate our effectiveness, people can see. And then, and I would not say, don't try to like be like other people. That's obviously not the right way to do it. But you being you, will attract more people like you. So don't be surprised by that. And if you want to have a more diverse customer base, side note, have more people in your content. So for example, one of the things that I would like to do this year, we're trying to get our schedule to work out, but I would like to do interactivity with me and Layla doing more live streaming together. Because we'll probably get women and men, and married couples. So we'll kind of get like all three of those, because we're together. So just telling you like what I'm doing. Okay, next is better targeting. So more avatar specific content. So how do you fix targeting with your content? So some of you guys are like, I get tons of views, but my leads suck. Right, or like the people that I have in my audience are super poor, totally reasonable. It's probably because you make content for poor people, and you're surprised that you have poor people in your audience. So if you want to make content for rich people, or attract rich people, then make rich people content. So one of the big shifts that you have to have is going from social media to interest media. That's the big change. And so I want you to write this one sentence down, and I want you to like circle it. The content is the targeting. The content is the targeting. If you want to reach 35 year old men, then make stuff for 35 year old men. And then the algorithm will show it to 35 year old men. If you make a national funny meme, that has no target because everybody thinks it's funny, which means that you will get an audience of everybody, which is a really shitty way to get leads. So if I go across the street and get slapped by a grandma, or I slap a grandma, or getting a slap fight with a grandma, I'll bet you that that video will go viral. It also will probably not get me good leads. And so you want to make stuff your avatar likes, and the algorithm will show it to them. And alternatively, think about this way, if you make stuff you hate, you will get people you hate. Right, and I'll say a fun one is like, sometimes people have like a channel like How to Save Money, and they tell everybody not to spend money, and then they're surprised that they can't sell stuff to that audience when they gathered everybody around and said, don't buy anything, right? So you want to be cognizant of the audience you're building, and ideally build an audience of people that you like so that you don't have to pretend to be somebody you're not. And so just be you and make stuff that you want to make. And then the people who will eventually get shown that stuff, you won't have to fake it for them to like you. And so I do have this, I will make a comment on memes in general, which is that I do think there was a place for making kind of like meme content, which is just only when it's specific to your industry, and not like the core pillar of your content. Like you're not, you're likely, unless you're an entertainer, which is not most of you, you probably want to make content, like if there's a, like Layla and I made a collab one yesterday of like, when you want to, you know, when you're in a yapping mood and he wants to work or something, because that's relevant for us, we work together, we're married. Like it's relevant and I have a ton of people in my audience who are married or have a significant other who, and they work and they have that dynamic, and so it's funny for them and so they'll share it, whatever. That works for us. Now there's tons of other memes that don't make any sense for us to do. And so those are the memes that you wouldn't want to duplicate. And so I want you to break a couple of beliefs around this, around like how big your audience needs to be in order to generate leads from content. All right, and the reason this is so important is that like, once you realize that this is about interest media, not social media, the likes and the views and all that stuff should matter to you dramatically less. Because I know that whenever I put out content that I think is really good, I'll usually get texts from people who I actually respect, and they're saying like, that was awesome. And it usually is like a mid to low performer, but I know it was really good. And so the right people will see it. And so here's three different businesses. So registered dietician that's doing over a million dollars off of, well, she was at $5,000, now she's at $7,500. So Amy Plano, she just teaches registered dieticians how to bill insurance. That's her whole niche. That's all she does. And like look at her average engagement. She gets like nine to 15 likes per post. All right, I'm bringing this up to you because like you probably think that your stuff doesn't get views and that it's worthless. And you're wrong, you're just wrong. As long as you make sure that the content you're making is for the right type of avatar, you actually get super engaged people because they know you're a small, they know you're a real person. But like this isn't crazy. Like this is a tens of millions a year business, maybe a hundred million a year.
