Like that's not what the numbers say. And if you make content for your, the ideal customer avatar, you can make great returns on this. All right, now I'll give you a real talk for me. So my Instagram explore page has tiny comedians. They're very small people. Uh, just like not huge. I've got big comedians too, but I have smaller accounts. Welders, gym equipment, entrepreneur news, and then like quotes from entrepreneurs. Like that is, that is my explore page. That's the stuff that I see. And so many people that I follow have small audiences, especially like welders, gym equipment, tiny comedians, but I am in that audience. And so just don't judge your content by views. It's about quality per eyeball, not quantity of eyeballs. It's hard to make this pivot for yourself, but it is worth it. Like when I made the first cash cows episode, I got so many people reached out to me because they're like, oh my God, this is amazing. And I read the comments on those videos. If you guys like cash cows, please drop in the chat. Have you seen those, seen those, seen those episodes? Right. Those are a ton of work for us, but like you, y'all are my audience, right? And those videos, you can look at their performance, aren't that good. They're like mid, but the comments on them are insane. And so like, I know this is the right type of content for my audience. So even though they're going to underperform my normal videos, I'm going to make more of them because I like them and you like them. And so for me, I don't remember what I had for this last point. My audience has money, book launch versus others. Yeah, I don't remember where that one was. Okay. I have remembered all my other points. Okay, so this is my quick and dirty on building the brand. Six steps. Number one, determine the perfect avatar, not your current avatar, the perfect avatar. Who is that customer? Who's perfect? You look at all the ones you have, you say, this one's perfect. Who's that one? All right, pull this one out, think about their name. Great. Number two, figure out what they want or what they're suffering from, what they need help with. And there's usually hundreds of things if they have like, oh, they want to make more money. Duh, if it's B2B or they want to lose weight. Duh, but what are the hundred other things that help them do that? Right? How do they start their morning? How can they eat this instead of that? How can they start the first workout without getting sore? There's a zillion things you can do that lead to them losing weight or them making money. Obviously, every business owner here wants to make more money. If I just said, make more money, not very valuable. So you have to break it down, like chunk down levels. There's unlimited amounts when you get into the microcosms, right? There's tons of content to make. So whenever you're like, oh, I don't know what to make it about. Just think smaller. Like just think in the margin. Like that's where all the cool content is. Just zoom in again. Just zoom in again, right? So who are they? Perfect person, fair they want. Make content that helps them get it. Remember I talked about power? Give them instructions on how to go what they want. Number four, show how you have the things they want. That you are like them in any proof that what you say is true has happened outside the real world. Five, ask them if they want your help with cause to action. Six, do this as much as you can without getting bored or letting negative comments stop you. Real. Best way to find out more about your perfect avatar, Mike, by the way, read Go to the Lost Chapters book and read your first avatar and outlines the entire process in detail. All right, now. Once we have all of stuff in our content, we have to repurpose across multiple platforms to get even more. So the secret to volume is having an easy way to make content. This is the secret. So everything that I have starts on X and the reason for that is because I'm a writer first and so I want to have a low friction way to shoot out ideas. Layla is not a writer first. She's a talker first. And so Layla has everything start on podcasts. That's her native style. And so for you, think about whether you're an audio person, you're a video person, you're a written person, whatever is easier for you, find a platform that allows you to do that first so that you can shoot moments of content in the moment. Right, because your motivation to make content, you probably have seen this, goes up and down very quickly. And so I want when those moments come, like everyone on my team knows, if I say something kind of pithy, I'm like, that was kind of good and I'll just shoot it out. And that tiny little process has honestly been the seed for everything we have. So I would encourage you to find that one thing that is the least amount of friction for you to create. The lowest, like don't worry about other formats, don't worry about platforms. Like X is not the best platform. Like if I could pick for my love or platform, it would be YouTube, but that's not me. That's not low effort for me. And so X is the easiest way for me to get something out quickly that has worked well. Some people it's like, I just want to go live, fine, go live. Like whatever works for you. And so here's kind of my levels of making content for you. So level one, make as much as you can. Level two is make as much as you can plus match it with proven hooks. So that's where you do a little bit of pre-work. Level three, find proven hooks and then you make content that matches those hooks. Level four, build the machine of automatically promoting you in your native com, Jesus. Build the machine of automatically promoting you in your native format to engineer virality. So what does that mean? That means how can I prepare my live streams so that all my prompts are high tested tweets or concepts that I already know convert? So how can I structure my stream in a way? So if we do call ins, how can we get it so each of the conversations begins with a hook? How can we do that? Right? That's where you start engineering your environment to maximize likelihood of virality. So my closing statement on the organic side is that if you're not as good as you want, you're probably just not doing enough. And if you have a service business, which 80% of you guys do, there's two types of reinvestment. So obviously software, it's like you hire more developers and you just keep trying to grow. You can't e-commerce some of the, the investment can be inventory. But if you're in a service business and you have this excess cash at the end of the year and you're like, I wanna reinvest in the business because I want this to grow faster, what do you do? You know what it is? It's two things. You reinvest in brand and reinvest in talent. That means that you hire really smart people that are smarter than everyone you currently have so they can help you build the future. And so what I have found is you will, okay, this is the best analogy. I'm gonna use this probably for the rest of my life because I love it so much. Many of you are trying to make decisions about your business like you're trying to make dinner. Let me explain. So you're going over the fridge, looking what's inside and saying, huh, based on what I have in here, what I wanna have for dinner, right? When in reality, you should just look at the world and ask, what the fuck do I wanna eat? And then go get the things that you need to make that meal. And so what happens is when you think about what's in the fridge, as your limitation, you have a very constrained thinking process. And so part of the reason that your business isn't growing as fast as you want is because you're only having four or five ingredients. You only have four or five good people to work with. There's just not a lot of ingredients here to make a great dinner. And so if you ask the question of what do I wanna eat? For real, not based on this, but what do I wanna eat? Then you can think much more broadly and then your reinvestment of, okay, well, if I wanna eat that, these are the associations I need to build my brand. These are the types of people I need to attract and this is how I'm gonna do it. So I just wanted to have this as a quote, kind of like closing thought around organic. In 2025, what that translates to is that brand will be content and documenting aspirational achievements for your avatar. Why is it that Land Rover will be able to show someone, somebody going off-roading in the snow or whatever, why do they show that? They show that because if you see that and you actually believe it, you think, well, if it can handle all those rocks, it can probably deal with two inches of snow on the highway and get me to my place safely. Why is it that Arcterics and North Face will give free jackets or whatever to the people who wanna climb the summit of Everest? Because you figure, you know, soccer mom says, well, if it kept them warm on the mountaintops of Everest, it'll keep me warm from the car to the door when it's a little chilly outside. So we have to achieve things that are orders of magnitude greater than the aspirations of our avatars so they can reverse that into the believability of, you know what, I think if it works all the way up there it'll probably work for me. If this guy was able to do a $100 million launch in three days, he can probably help me take my business from $1 million to $2 million. He can probably help me take my business from $10 million to $20 million. That's the idea. We want to think about what their aspirations are and then do unreasonable amounts of work, document it so that you now make that reinforcement of, wow, that's exactly what this avatar wants and I'm going to demonstrate that I have documented this whole thing and I can give instructions so they can follow it. Cool. Okay. So that was our organic section. So now we move on to outreach. All right, so this is the third. There's three buckets, you got ads, you got organic, you got outreach. I would say like the book is kind of an exception for that, but like, it's typically free or a very low cost thing. Um, I would say that an attraction offer, that's a little bit of a difference. But the attraction offer, the way that I imagine it from a like a mechanistic perspective is that you are wrapping something that is already kind of fully formed with a different incentive. And so like buy X get Y free, uh, a giveaway, um, you know, when your money back, these are all things that like you have the core deliverable, but it makes like the, the mechanism around it makes the thing more desirable. Whereas a lead magnet, it's usually just the fact that it's free or super low cost. Like that is the thing that makes it desirable, uh, relative to its value. So this is like most common practice. The second way, which I think is wildly underestimated, uh, are underutilized is use outreach to get affiliates. So like it's like, if I was going to do cold outreach to get like high networked individuals, it would make way more sense for me to do cold outreach to accounting practices who have many high networked individuals and then do deals with them, right? Like try and use a lower leverage path to go to a higher leverage person rather than, rather than go a low leverage path to a one on one sale. I mean, nothing wrong with the one on one thing. It's just like, it's just harder, right? And then three, um, and this again is not nearly enough talked about, but I've said this at the beginning, which is that all businesses are demand constrained. And then you've got the tools that help you with all of them. All right. So breaking this down, getting leads. So number one, it's like find where the people hang out. So you can buy leads, you can scrape data, or you can manually hunt all three of these work. Obviously buying leads and scraping data can be more scalable, but if you are poorer, then manually hunting works fine too. So just get good at it, then expand. Now, once you have your, your list of leads, your sources for leads, um, the whole goal is to make it not look automated, even though it is automated. All right. Um, the, we wanted to, like, I was once up like super magic, uh, tell people how much it is and, and make sure that they agreed to the biggest objection when they, when they apply. So biggest objection for people coming to a workshop is that they don't want to travel. And so we just said, Hey, are you willing to travel in the next 90 days or the next 120 days? They say yes. We're like, Hey, the tickets, this much money. Is that okay? Yes. And so like we've already just like, like the amount that, that like decreased in applications, but increased in sales efficiency. So we netted more sales overall, number one, but the efficiency per rep dramatically improved. So we actually just can get more sales per rep. And so like, these are the types of friction that I talk about in terms of like improving, uh, throughput in a funnel that are typically counterintuitive. So a great one for timing. I had a friend who had a, um, a very large business that, that just received lots of applications and they found out that 95% of their sales, uh, they could correlate with an answer on one of their questions, which was, um, would like, if you were selected or if it was a good fit, would you be willing to start, uh, as soon as seemingly possible tomorrow or next week? And when someone said as soon as possible or tomorrow, they were the 95% and then like zero sales came from next week. And so they just started immediately disqualifying people when they just selected that they didn't even care about anything else because those people just never bought. So the more data like this you collect, which, um, I wasn't planning on saying this, but I'm going to say it anyways. Um, some of you guys have asked the questions. Think for high value targets, LinkedIn outreach sales navigator. Um, this one I wanted to highlight. Some of you guys go for really high value targets. So you're looking at like real estate investors or looking construction firms or whatever. Um, in my experience, going to as many of these conferences as humanly possible works very well. So I'm going to give you a very tactical, uh, strategy to do this. So number one, and Sharon talked about this briefly on the, the mega live. So number one is you want to come up with a speaking fee. So let's say that you're speaking fees, $25,000. It doesn't matter cause you're going to wave it. So let me explain. So you're going to say, Hey, um, I can speak to your vet. I have a $25 for speaking fee. Um, and if you're like, how do I get, you know, invitations to these types of vets? Well, making content helps. Also, you can hire an agent that does it. That helps too. Um, there's different, like, and as soon as you get some clips of you on stage, and you get posted organically, you will start getting inquiries. Um, and I would encourage you to take the ones that are free and whatnot. And you do those things so that one, you can get content to, you can quit pro crow. You can flip those into things that are far more valuable. So what would you trade a speaking fee for? So number one is you say, Hey, uh, you have this event that's coming up that has a thousand attendees. Um, I would like to wave my speaking fee in exchange for me being able to get that list of attendees. That's option one. On, if someone does that for you, what a gift. It's like, they probably spent $250,000 advertising the event and you're going to get that $250,000 gift. Now thing is, is that incremental cost for them is basically nothing. Um, now some of them might be like, I don't want to give you that list. Fine. A step down from that is saying, Hey, can I just send one email to that list? It's your list. I'll give you the email. You can read it and you can send it. And then that way you can just skim off the top and you do it after the events over. All right. So you don't do it upfront. You go speak, you get edified, then you send the email. So that's the second way. Now, third thing, let's say that they do neither of those things, but you still get to speak great. And let's say that they don't want you to pitch anything. Also great. So the way that you do this in a really elegant way is you do something called a survey close. So survey clothes works like this. You say, Hey, were these slides great? They'll say yes. And you say, Hey, if you want, just fill out your, like here's the links so that you can get all my slides and my free thingy. And so go there and you can answer a couple of questions. And it triages saying, Hey, would you like to find out more about this thing? Or were you only here for the free slides? They say, I want to find out more, which then you could, they can book directly to a phone call or if you're at a massive event, then you'd push them to a webinar, which you then pitch on the webinar and then push them to phones or push them to buy. That's how that works. Now that survey close method also works for getting talent. So if you're like, man, I'm really supply constrained and I want to go find out where the types of people that I need are, then you can just serve it close an audience of attorneys or accountants or whatever to get a specific role that you need. Okay. Now, I will just give you a couple of quick tidbits on, you can also swap it for a booth at any of these conferences or events. And the key at making these booths work is that you have to have an amazing lead magnet giveaway, just like always. Right. So some people go to these events and they're like, I went to a, you know, I, I bought a booth and it didn't work. Well, well, just like saying, I bought an ad and it didn't work. It's like, duh, if you buy a bad ad, it's not going to work. If you have a bad booth strategy, it's not going to work either. All right. So thing number one, you want to buy booths that are on the right side when you walk in the door. Why? Cause 70% of people turn right. And so they'll typically price these two the same, but this one's going to get more than twice the traffic. So buy the one on the right. Number one. Number two, uh, when you, when you have this, this booth strategy, um, there's typically other speakers that are there, have a little mini podcast setup. So you can invite the speakers to record a podcast with you. They're already there. They might as well get the content, but you, people are going to see the speakers who have already been edified and you, who hasn't been edified talking next to them with the headphones on, people gather around. What is that? It's live content and people are around your booth. It becomes something that's far more interesting and you get a ton of content with all these industry leaders. And then by default, you get elevated because you have their status that's reflecting onto you. Third thing, what is the giveaway? So you're going to give some sort of amazing thing away. Obviously you want to be curious. Um, people can line up and get like, uh, like a free key or something like that to a key to the vault. Everyone gets one. The way they get it is they give you their information and you give them a big basket of keys. One of the keys is the right one and they can try it out. Very fun. Do it so that if it's a multi-day event, put the correct key in on the last day. Right. And then you'll find the winner on the last day. Um, and then the, the, the price can be whatever you want it to be, but people will just think it's cool and they'll try it out and while they're waiting in line or after they give you their information, you now have leads. So pretty cool. Right. I thought that was pretty cool. Now, uh, next piece is volume. And this is just kind of an understanding here. I, the amount of times that I talk to business owners and they're like, yeah, I get like a third of my leads from events and I'm like, okay, uh, how many events are you doing a year? And they're like, I do two a year. Am I great? So then I say, okay, how many a week are you going to do? And then they're like, it's like, yeah, dude. Like if you get half your business or a 30 year business from two a year, that means every time you do one of these, you get one sixth of your business being 17% of your revenue per event. So what if we multiplied that by 25, which means you do one a week. Well, 25 times one sixth is a lot. That's a four X, right? So if you four extra revenue, just by doing that, not bad. So just like, and the other thing that'll happen is you'll start getting good at it too. And then the more events you speak at the more events will ask you to speak. Crazy. I know. So that's, you get more leads. I just wanted to zone in on the, on the conference style stuff because for, uh, many of you guys, that will be a really good way for the ones who have like really high value targets that are more sporadic in terms of how you close customers. That will work great for you guys. Now, better targeting with AI, uh, listen, Richmond. All right. So I'll give you a couple of stories here. So I think I tell the story in the first avatar chapter in lost chapters. But when I met up with the head of pricing from Vista private equity, which is like a gazillion dollar fund, um, I think they would have like $300 billion, just like an enormous fund, uh, for software. And their entire model is triple the value of the company in three years. And so I, the guy got off stage and he said, Hey, do you want to know how we do it? And I was like, yeah, I do want to know how you do it. He said, so we basically do 80, 20 on the entire customer base. And so what that is, is he said, we know our customers are sorry, are the businesses we're requiring customers better than they do. And so we do this big analysis on it to figure out which, which customers are the 80 and which ones are the 20. And so then what we do is we then say, all right, we're going to cut all of the streams and messaging and channels where the 80% come from. And then we're going to put five X more attention or where the 20% comes from. Now, the reason this is interesting is that imagine you have your five, let's say you've got one fifth, two fifths, three fifths, four fifths, five fifths. Right. So they say, we're going to get rid of all of these. And then we're going to fill it back up with this top stream. Now, the reason this is important is that you already have the infrastructure in place to support this many customers. But when you get the same amount of customers that are all worth five times as much, you five X the business. Kind of interesting. And he said, so fundamentally we make that, we make that change. And then we match the prices and the messaging to this better lead and then double down on those channels. And so the process that they go through is number one, they think about who are these people? So this is the quick and dirty of the, of many of you, the issue of like, I need to get cheaper leads is actually you need to get better leads so that you can get better returns rather than I need cheaper leads. This is the, how do we get better returns question? So first is who are the demographics? Right. Next is what are they doing? These are quantifiable requirements. All right. So for example, for us, Jim launched, we figured out when we looked at all of our customers that the, the, the gyms, they had one employee assigned to least, at least 30 members were the ones that were like five times more likely to be successful with our stuff. And so instead of that saying, Oh, these people were more successful, we just made that a requirement upfront. They have to have these quantifiable requirements. Right. Now the next is what did they do? So this is a really important one. So what are the activation behaviors? All right. So for us, pre-purchase, we found out that 78% of customers who came in consumed at least two long form pieces of content. And so in hearing that, I thought, okay, so why don't I take cold people and then force the consumption of two long form pieces of content to get them to be the same way as people who buy? And it turns out that worked pretty good. And a VSL, by the way, or webinar would count as one of those things. Right. So if you have like a lead magnet that you know people consume plus a long form thing that they consume after that, like another hour, if you have two or three hours with someone, you can usually sell like a very expensive thing. Like it doesn't actually take nearly as much time as you think. The next is a post purchase. So what are the activation behaviors after someone was made to purchase that makes them more likely to buy again, stick or pay even more. Right. So for us, it was, they had to get one high ticket sale within the first seven days. Now for you, it's going to be a different thing. But the idea, if we put all of this together, is we say, okay, this is the right type of customer. This is what they are quantifiable. So we're going to, we're going to screen for that on the front end. These are the things that these people did prior to buying that made it highly likely they would purchase from us. And then immediately after buying, this is the process that maximized the likelihood of success. That's where we're building here. Hopefully you're taking that in because that's, that's real. That's how that works. And then finally we have, okay, are there some sources that are bringing us way more of these good leads than others? And if so, let's double down on those. So let me give you a real, a real world example of this. So it could either be channels that bring in better customers or it could also be lead magnets or offers that bring in better customers. This is a really big one for a lot of you guys. You don't even know it. So scaling roadmap for us is a lead magnet. A come to a workshop is a direct offer. Right. Now scaling roadmap has higher row as on a longer timeline. The workshop has a higher row as on a shorter timeline. And so it makes more sense for us to get more people on the scaling roadmap because we make more money from it, even though it takes longer to get here. So we're matching the lead magnet that has the highest row as to the correct customer that has these quantifiable requirements. We're getting them to consume a process that maximizes the likelihood of them purchasing. And then after they purchase, we have them go through the process that maximizes the likelihood of success. That's what we do. And the nice thing about this is that you don't have to do anything right now to figure this out. You just look at your existing customers. You can answer this through literally observation and surveys. You can do this now. So in terms of qualifying leads, it's like, OK, how do I do this from a survey perspective? I think having more information required on the intake is wildly undervalued. So this is our scaling workshop meeting often. So you can just see it. That's what it is. That's not my phone number. So relax. So how to get this data? So obviously we put bants on all forms yet again as a reminder on your opt-in forms. Don't be afraid of adding friction to get the information you're near need because it rarely drops sales. So you may get more expensive leads, but your cost to occur customers will typically go down. Real. You can also get this information on intake calls and onboarding. Right? So every time you touch a customer, like not only do we provide value, we want to be extracting data. Right? And we do this so we can build our models. We get very good at predicting what they want, what they need and when they're going to buy next. And so I'll give you guys a very cool free nugget. OK. Now I had a book launch. And I added these two fields, right? Which is when with the amount of spend that we're doing is significant amounts of friction, right? That's like every, every one of these things that we asked for when you're talking about four or five million dollars in spend is going to be dropping the amount of people who signed up by like 10, 20, 30, 50,000 leads. So it's a lot, right? So one thing that was really important for me was that I wanted to make sure that I had business owners coming to this event, that this was not for like newbies per se. Obviously people in comments afraid, but like I wanted to have more business owners. And so my way of having a leading indicator on my ad quality was that I asked who here was a business owner when people registered. And so we had 65% of people who registered for the money models launch identified as a business owner. 65%, right? Which is pretty crazy. Now I think part of that was because the nature of the content was a cash conversion cycle and increasing cash flow, which is something that somebody who doesn't have a business doesn't even think about. And I think so the very nature of the topic was very like money and cash flow oriented, which is more of a business owner topic. Now here is the very, very sexy little nugget for you. So you see that little checkbox right there where it says, I want a VIP ticket. Who wants to guess how much extra money that brought into the business? So we split tests, I want to say like eight or nine different variations of this checkbox. And it was one of those like I woke up in the middle of the night with this idea and then like slacked the team and was like, Hey, let's do this. And so what the idea was was it was just based on pre-suasion, right? Which is, is there a way that I can just psychologically bias someone to make them more likely to buy? And the answer was yes. And so that checkbox did nothing. It did literally nothing. It changed not like literally the checkbox that had no change in what happens there in the entire funnel. But it was like 80 or 85% of people check that box. Here's why this is interesting. By adding that checkbox, we decreased the number of leads by like, I want to say like 6%, something like that. But it increased the take rate on the upsell on the next page by I think over 20%. And so I don't remember the, those two numbers, but I know the net increase overall of earnings per view, which is what I would care about, which is of every, every eyeball that got to the page, how much extra was made? It was an absolute 16% increase. Wild. From a checkbox that did nothing. Because it pre-framed the person to buy and then you had commitment and consistency. And so some of you guys have things like this on your funnels and we just have to think to ourselves and no, the checkbox was not mandatory. It was completely optional. It literally did nothing. But just by getting someone to say, I want a VIP ticket. And then on the next page, it says, want to know how to get a VIP ticket. We made people more likely to buy. And I don't know the revenue numbers. Maybe, maybe Lewis might know them, but do you know with how much more valuable the VIPs were versus not VIP? Was it? He's pulling it. Okay. I'll give you the exact numbers in a second. Um, but it was a lot. And so anything that I could do to get more people to pre-order a book. Um, I did. And so I thought you guys would think this was cool. So one is that post event. We know all the people were business owners and we know how much money they made because they said so. And so these leads become significantly more valuable to my business because even if they don't do anything at the event, at least I have good lead data. Cause I can't, my business does, I can't like, we have too much volume. So we have, you know, minimum, basically requirements for people to get here is usually around 500,000 ish minimum, uh, for them to come to an L, L one, you know, event here, a median is usually between like one and four million, um, for the hundred people in the room. So now let's move on to cadence. So, um, this comes down to split testing in general. And this really does work across all three of these, whether it's content, whether it's ads and whether it's outreach, I would say this is more specifically towards outreach and ads because you have more consistent inflow, whereas constant can be spiky and the nature of the traffic can change based on the content that went viral or that, you know, hit some air. Um, I just want to give you guys like my testing cadence for how we do this internally. So we run split tests every one, like every week, and we usually run one to two per week and I'll explain, um, how they, how they work. So in my opinion, this needs to be the second thing you bring up in your marketing. So the first thing you're going to bring up is like, how many leads do we get? How many are qualified? Like what's the main KPI? The next thing that we talk about every single week is where the, where the, where the split tests that we ran or the winners. We do this every single week, every Monday, we have a market meeting. All right. And so what we do is we say, where's the point of greatest leverage? And so the point of greatest leverage is going to be typically the lowest relative percentile in our flow, uh, relative to benchmark. So remember I started with this earlier. We had our midway. Hold on. Hold on. Hold on. I feel like a Friday to anyone. It was like a Friday to me. Okay. Uh, we have these percentages. Well, whichever one of these is the low relative to the benchmark is going to be the, where we're going to say, okay, we're going to attack right here. You got to lose. Okay. VIP was, yeah. So that little check box pushed people to in a bucket of leads that became 10 to 11 times more valuable. So we could, like that little check made increase the likelihood that someone would take an action that then made them 10 to 11 times more valuable as a customer. So like these tiny little hinges can sometimes swing big doors. All right. Back to this. So, uh, the point of greatest leverage is the lowest performing thing relative to benchmark. So the next question we ask is, what is a good alternative test? So I want to give you our testing methodology, which I got for Mr. Bees, which is very good. It works for paid ads just as much as it works for YouTube thumbnails and packaging. All right. You will remember this. This is how it works. Orange apple banana. All right. Step one. So we come up with three versions that are wildly different. One orange, one apple, one banana, three completely different concepts for, uh, you know, like a homepage or an opt-in or a headline or whatever we're testing, we have three completely different versions. All right. We run that as test one. Then we go green apple, red apple, yellow apple. So once we know which of the three, uh, orange apple banana one, we then say great of that subcategory, we make three smaller permutations within it. And so that gives you a nine box of three by three in terms of testing variations. Um, and this cause listen, like it's easy to say, Hey, just test everything under the moon. The problem with tests is that it takes resources to do tests and the cost of tests is the traffic cost to do it and the losses in sales you otherwise would have made on your control. Right. And so for us, it's like we test a percentage, not the entirety of the traffic. And if it wins, then we do a true 50, 50 split test. And then if it holds, then it becomes the control. All right. And so typically like, hopefully you guys like that. I actually think it's just so simple and it works great. Um, and so typically we'll have one that's at step one and one that's at step two every week. And again, this is not a rule of law. This is just a trend that I've observed for us. All right. And this, again, this is for all types of marketing outreach ads organic, but most of it is going to be funnel, uh, funnel specific, uh, type split test work well. And, uh, the organic version of this is like Instagram has the trial reels, which hopefully you guys have heard about, um, trial reels wildly under, under used. But right now they just put a limit. So it's five permutations of a trial reel, um, that you can put out per day. And the trial reels themselves, you want to make sure that they're obviously all different, but the way to do it is just have one meat and have five different hooks for them and you'll see wildly different results and I'll show you just how powerful nailing that first three seconds really is. Okay. So, uh, those are the things that we do to get more leads from our outreach attempts. We make better offers, better lead man. Cause like normal, we do more outreach using the tools. We buy more leads from brokers, software, manual effort, or we run the events process. We get better targeting, uh, by qualifying our leads and making sure that the leads that we're getting into the people who are more likely to buy and increase the likelihood that they become the type of lead who is going to be most valuable to our business. And we run a split test cadence on all components of the business on a weekly cadence using apple, orange, banana. Now part four, and I'll, I'll, I'll end off on this. Um, Layla was talking to, uh, a mentor and she was saying that she was having issues with something and basically that she had settled. And the mentor said something to her that like, and I, it moved her and it certainly moved me. She said, are you trying? Are you moving mountains? And I just, I just really like, I just really like that. Which is sorry that all my bullets are all on the screen at the same time. It really destroys my, my nice timing here. Um, but. When I wanted to build out all of the funnels and all the VSCLs that had to do this launch, like I could have very reasonably said this is going to take weeks and weeks and weeks and weeks and weeks. But I was like, what does it actually take to build all of the materials for this? And I figured it would take like eight guys and like 48 hours all locked into a room. And so that's basically what I did. I just got the eight, you know, craziest guys, you know, stemmed up to the gills to stay in a room locked in, no one was allowed to come in except for food and water and the whole company. And we built out all the funnels, all the video sales letters, all the, the connection points. And we got the whole thing basically live in 24 or 26 working hours. So they had a timer because I wanted to prove the point that I know that like, like if we hadn't done it that way, it would have been like week after week after week and be like, oh, we're moving the project forward or moving the project forward. And I was like, we can get all of this done if all the decision makers are in the room for two days. And so that's what we did. And so I bring this up because it's like so many times we have these goals of like, I want to get more leads or I want to scale the company. And it's like, are you really removing all doubt? Are you removing every variable that you possibly could? Are you trying? Are you moving fucking mountains? Right. And I'll tell you the story. I had a, I had a client way back in the day. His name was Derek. Um, and honestly, it was just like a mediocre to shitty client. And he was always complaining and whining about like nothing's working, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, right? And like fast forward a few months, he became one of the highest performing gyms. So I got on the phone with him. I was like, what changed? And he was like, me. I was like, well, tell me more about that. He said, you know, I was just like, I was always looking for a reason to say that something wouldn't work. Like that I had like a special scenario. He's like, and then I just realized that like, I was basically like staying poor and staying unsuccessful to prove myself right, rather than just like go all in and just really try to make things work. And as soon as I did that, I made way more. And I thought about that a lot. He ended up becoming, you know, he actually ended up starting working for Jim Montier's later. Um, and I kind of want to like tell that story partially for you guys, because like you're going to be right either way. Like you're going to be ready to the right. I know I saw the chat. You guys have been like super awesome, which like you guys are, you guys are dope. Um, but like you're, you're going to be right. Like if you're like, oh my God, I'm going to squeeze the hell of the ROI here. I'm going to use, there's so many things I'm going to use and deploy my business. It's like, yeah, you're right. And it'll probably work great for you. And then for somebody else, it's like, you know, Alex didn't actually show me how to click every one of the buttons. You're right. You're right. It didn't work for you. Right. It's not. Um, because like if you need someone to do that, you want someone to just give you, to just give you money. Like that's what you want. Is that you want it to get regressed to the level where you want no effort. And I will promise, like I'll tell you this, is that many people will promise you that no one will actually do it. Um, and so it takes people a different amount of time to realize that you were just going to have to figure something's out. And so if you get the direction correct, um, and you can avoid some pitfalls along the way, you will do significantly better. And I would just think about all my decisions directionally rather than absolutely, which I talked about at the, at the big launch event. Um, but I really meant that. Um, and so I had a guy who was out here, um, Brent mentioned him earlier, uh, needed to scale, um, basically needed to double the sales team. And so, I mean, I brought up the story of him of like, and he was like, oh, this can be hard. This can be a lot of work or whatever. And he's a great guy. I'm, I'm probably exaggerating a little bit, but, um, for dramatic effect. But I was like, if you're, if your daughter was going to get taken from you in 30 days, unless you hired 40 sales guys, I was like, do you think you could hire 40 sales guys? He was like, yeah. And I was like, right, then why don't you? Like what would you do? Like what would you do? If you needed to go from what you're currently doing to 20 pieces of content a day, or someone would die, would you? Yes. Then why don't you? Like you're willing to do this to save someone else, but you're not willing to do that level of effort to save yourself. And I find that odd. And so we had a portfolio company that we had to stand up a sales team and we had to send up a 50 person sales team in 90 days and made a YouTube video about it. But I'll tell you what we did. It was really straightforward. We. Hired 10 different recruiters and we told all of them they could bring as many as they wanted to us and we'd pay this flat rate. And then we spun up our own recruiting and maxed out our ad spend across multiple platforms and we ended up hiring like 80 and kept 50. And that process from end to end took 90 days. So like, let's say it was $5,000 per sales rep. It cost us 250 grand and that team brought in like 5 million a month. It's like, what are we talking about? Right. And so I think Jensen Wang was talking about how Elon built some data center. Forgive me for whatever the technical jargon is, but he built something AI tech center thing in 19 days. That's supposed to take like 18 months and he did it basically 30 times as fast. And Elon has this algorithm that I really think about a lot because it's so incredibly useful and it applies to everything in business when you're trying to fix a process, which is question, delete, optimize, accelerate, automate. I won't read this part to you, but basically like, why are we doing this? And if you find out that there's no point in doing it, don't do it. Right. Optimize my teams of nodding their head behind the cameras because they've heard me say this so many times. Once you've deleted everything, then you optimize what's left and find the points of greatest leverage with the fewest actions that are required. And then you ask the question, when do you think you can get this done by? And then you ask, why can't you get it done today or within the next two hours? And then the follow up question is, what is it that you're working on right now that is more important than this? And if there is nothing, then this becomes the biggest priority. Right. We just have to ask these questions. And sometimes they say something. I'm like, no, that is more important. Let's do this, do that, do this second. So you can do this by the end of the day. And so when you get into these like operations modes, like I just, if this matters to you, then make it matter. Right. And then once you've excited the timeline, then you can just automate it. Right. Which would be great. But. Big picture, like if you know what you, if you know what you need to do, then I, I've always, I've always struggled why to understand why people don't do it. If you know how to do something under the context of, you know, your family member would die, then you still already know how to do it. You just like choose to not try as hard. I just don't get it. And I'll give you a real world use case of this algorithm. So when we were going through this process, kind of like reengineering the media team, I asked a question because we just bought all these really nice cameras. And I was like, so what's the issue? Like what, why can't we get stuff out? Like today, like I film it now. Why can't we get it out within an hour? It was like, well, I mean, shoot, it takes us, you know, eight hours to upload this footage. And I was like, okay, why does it take us eight hours to upload this footage? They're like, oh, because we have these fancy cameras. And I was like, why did we get fancy cameras? If it just makes it longer to up, to upload stuff, they're like, well, because they're fancy. And I was like, how does fancy cameras have anything to do with how well a piece of content does? They're like, who doesn't? And I was like, then why do we use them? And so you can see how these meetings go, right? And so then I created a new thing called Mission 24, which was that when it from, from the time we filmed something to the time it goes out is 24 hours. All right. Cause I had something was like, I was coming in there like it's two to three week lead types for some of my YouTube videos. And I was like, this is dog shit. This is ridiculous. Why does this take that long? I was like, if a girl on, on Twitch can have an only fans and she can have three camera angles using her toes to, to swap between things. I was like, we certainly have the resources and the skills and the intelligence to figure out how to do something comparable. Right? Like why? And like her setup costs a hundred dollars and somehow mine costs 400,000. How does, how does that work? Right. And so the next big, you know, breakthrough that we found out was that like 80% of the work in afterwards was in post-production. So that was like eight to 16 hours per video. And we were able to solve that by just preparing more. And so 60 minutes of pre was worth like 80 minutes of pre. 18 hours of post. So we're like, let's do more prep. Okay. If we do more prep and we don't use fancy cameras, then all of a sudden we go from a two week lead time, two day lead time. Great. If we do that, we aid x the speed of our ability to go from input to output. And so I bring this up because most of marketing. Comes down to this. You have an input output equation. And so we just have to optimize this box. That's it. And so I'll give you one more little, little tidbit, which is that for those of you who do make content on a regular basis, making piece of content plus the CTA at the end, the CTA, we actually use AI for it a lot of times. So the AI video stuff isn't good enough to my opinion to do like the, the full ad, but if you're just doing a CTA at the end, Hagen icon, there's, there's ton of different video, um, AIs that are out there. You can just have them. Like you just, I have like 32 different settings of like t-shirts, hats, no hat, beard, short, long, inside, outside, whatever, sitting, standing, walking, iPhone, selfie style, professional camera, all these are permutations. And then we can just type in what the CTA is. And then the avatar says it. So it's now a really easy way for you to get more leverage on the work that you do. So this is like the process that we went through in terms of like, how do we accelerate it? How do we automate it? Like all of that stuff. And so I bring this up because I said I was going to land the plan. Like I took it off, which is that speed is the greatest strategic advantage. So if you think about it from the equal opposite perspective, like taking to the opposite extreme, you do nothing ever, right? You're so slow that nothing ever gets accomplished. And so the, the, the alternate version of this is like, you're so fast that no one even has an innovator's advantage because one second after they produce something, you already have it produced. And so the reason strategy is so important is that it unlocks speed is because it allows you to allocate all your resources to the great point, the point of greatest leverage. And so I'll tell you an example of this. So I had a home, a home flipping company. So they were doing, I think like 16, 17 million a year. So decent sized business. And so they came, they did an L three with me here, um, in Vegas, which is me with like 10 business owners. And in, in, in going through all of the stuff, the point of greatest leverage in the business is that they had a slow cash conversion cycle. And because of that, they were cash, cashflow constrained. And so they knew how to get the deals. They knew how to flip the properties. But because of how long it took from buying the properties to investing the properties to flipping and getting their profit, they would either have to raise a huge fund, which it wasn't like the best for that. Um, and they didn't want to dilute their equity and things like that, uh, or dilute the returns. It wouldn't be worth it. Um, or I just said, if you just went from, it takes you 90 days to flip this to 30 days to flip it, all your problems are solved. You can do three times the, the revenue that you currently are, you're no longer cashflow constrained and, and, and the initial reaction was like, well, I mean, there's no way we could do that. And I said, why? And so after we kept unplugging, like why, well, could you pay this guy more? Well, could you do this? By the end of it, it was like, we could totally do this. We could probably do it in 20 days. It was like, right. But the thing is, is that we have to start again, kind of like the refrigerator example of like, instead of like, what's in the fridge? Just asking the question of like, what would it take? What do I want to eat for dinner? Like if we wanted to be the fastest, what would we do? If we want to make it the easiest, what would we do? If we want to make the most guaranteed, the risk-free, what would we actually do? And taking, operating from that perspective. And the reason I'm bringing this up to quote, land the plane is that the big thing still matter the most, right? I, I can go through as many of the details and all these tactics and all these things that we want. And I know you guys got a bunch of those, a ton of different notes that you can take from it. But like the restaurant will not succeed if the food sucks. Right. You have to make a good sandwich. Like it doesn't matter how good your marketing is, because eventually the word of mouth will still catch up to you on the negative side. Cause negative word of mouth travels way faster, way broader than positive does. And so it takes a ton of time, a really consistent execution to get a decent reputation and it takes a very short amount of time of terrible, of terrible execution to get a very bad, very big reputation. And so I, I bring all this up because I want to ask the question, which is like, how hard are you really trying? And you'll be like, holy shit, like a third of our sales are coming from these, these tiny posts that we're making. And then you'll start getting exciting about it, excited about it. And so then you'll start posting as much as humanly possible. And the thing is, is that that never ends. And so branding gives you pricing power and demand. It gives you both of those things, which is the ultimate leverage in business. And so a few final thoughts. Um, why, I think I've always been able to generate more demand than I've been able to deliver on, um, just comes down to like the two statements of do epic shit and then talk about what you did. And I think that it's like very simple to say, very hard to do. And usually have to think much further out than most people do in terms of planning and how much effort goes into making things good. Um, and if we think about like, what is the purpose of doing all this stuff? The purpose of marketing is to shift the supply demand curve. So if we've supply and demand, right, if we shift the curve, right, um, then what's going to happen is we're going to get, so if demand goes up this way, right, then price will go up, right? So by doing this, we get more people to buy at a higher price. What we're trying to do with marketing is artificially shift the demand curve in our favor and have superior supply demand economics because we just pull supply towards us. And so the leverage that we try to create is to get as much as you can for what you put in, and that is the foundation of strategy. And so everybody here is listening to this has very limited resources and I went over zillions of different things that you can do to get more leads, except for the one person who asked if I could give a couple of tips on getting leads. Um, which is so great. But the biggest thing that you can do to get more leads is just do way more than you currently are. It's all I could say, like it's just, it's so much more. I mean, when I showed you PBDs doing nine longs a day, right? And Rams is doing four longs a day, just on his two main channels, not including the other seven channels he has, like, and you're like, I'm getting, you know, two longs out a month. It's like, yeah, like they're, they're literally doing like 200 times the advertising you're doing. And that's just on that one platform, not what they're doing across everything else. And so when you actually do the math, it's actually much more linear than you expect it to be. If you're like, okay, well, I post it, I want to give you guys some basic math here. So let's say you did one post a day, which is more than most of you do. Let's just say you did one post a day. It's like you did 365 posts. So I did 35,000. So I did a hundred times more than you. Well, we'll do more than a hundred million dollars this year. Obviously we did a hundred in the weekend, but like, like it's, like, it's not that far off. You just like, it really is a much more linear equation than you think it is.
